SIP Field Registry — Complete

All 1404 fields with definitions and examples, across 92 tables organized into 28 screens.

100% coverage — every field now has a definition, explanation, and example value.
42 fields renamed for clarity. Cyan-bordered cards show renames.

Organization Setup

4 tabs · 76 fields
🔑Org_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier assigned to each organization in the SI platform.
Should be stable, non-reusable, and unique across all platform records.
Example: ORG-0001
📝Org_Name
PrimaryDropdownText
Registered or commonly used name of the organization.
Use official brand or registered company name; avoid abbreviations unless standard.
Example: Alpine Crest Dairy
📝Legal_Entity_Type
InputDropdownText
Legal structure under which the organization operates.
Use a controlled list such as Private Limited, Public Limited, LLC, PLC, Partnership, Cooperative.
Example: Private Limited Company
📝Industry_Sector
InputDropdownText
Primary industry sector in which the organization operates.
Map to a standard sector taxonomy so benchmarking and aggregation remain consistent.
Example: Food & Beverage
📝Sub_Sector
InputText InputText
More specific industry classification within the sector.
Use a finer classification aligned to the selected sector.
Example: Dairy Products
📝HQ_City
InputText InputText
City where the organization's headquarters is located.
Capture the operational headquarters city, not branch or plant locations.
Example: Bengaluru
🔢HQ_Country
InputNumeric InputNumber
Country where the headquarters is located.
Use a standard country name from the platform master list.
Example: India
📆Founded_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the organization was established.
Store as a 4-digit year only.
Example: 2008
📝Parent_Company
InputText InputText
Name of the parent company, if the organization belongs to a group.
Leave blank if independent; otherwise use the exact parent entity name.
Example: Alpine Crest Group
💰Revenue_Band
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Annual revenue range used for segmentation and benchmarking.
Prefer standardized bands like <50M, 50M-250M, 250M-1B, >1B.
Example: 250M-1B USD
📝Employee_Band
InputDropdownText
Workforce size range used for organizational scale analysis.
Prefer standardized bands like 1-200, 201-1000, 1001-5000, 5001+.
Example: 1001-5000
📝Listed_Status
InputDropdownText
Whether the organization is publicly listed or privately held.
Use standard values such as Listed, Unlisted, Private, Subsidiary.
Example: Unlisted
📝CIN_or_RegNoRequired
InputText InputText
Corporate identification number, registration number, or equivalent legal ID.
Mandatory where legally available; helps external validation and deduplication.
Example: U15209KA2008PTC045678
📝Primary_Business
InputText InputText
Main business activity, offering, or operating model.
Describe the core business in one line, not the full portfolio.
Example: Manufacture and distribution of milk, cheese, yogurt, and value-added dairy products.
📝Website
InputText InputText
Official company website URL.
Use full URL format beginning with https://.
Example: https://www.alpinecrestdairy.com
📝Mission
InputText InputText
Core purpose of the organization and the value it aims to deliver to customers, employees, and stakeholders.
State why the organization exists in practical business terms. Keep it specific enough to guide workforce and capability planning.
Example: To deliver reliable, high-quality products and services through innovation, operational excellence, and skilled talent.
📝Vision
InputText InputText
Long-term aspirational future state the organization wants to achieve.
Describe what the organization aims to become over time. This should support strategic capability and transformation planning.
Example: To become the most trusted and future-ready enterprise in our industry through innovation, customer value, and workforce capability.
📝Values
InputText InputText
Core principles and beliefs that guide organizational behavior, decisions, and culture.
Capture the organization’s foundational values as a concise list or statement. These values can later align with leadership and behavioral competency design.
Example: Integrity, Innovation, Collaboration, Accountability, Customer Focus
📝Business_Objectives
InputText InputText
Key business goals the organization is trying to achieve over the planning horizon.
List the major measurable goals that shape workforce, capability, and operating priorities.
Example: Improve productivity, expand into new markets, strengthen customer retention, and build leadership depth.
📝Strategic_Priorities
InputText InputText
Major strategic focus areas that guide investment, transformation, and execution.
These are the top enterprise priorities that the SI platform should align skills, workforce, and learning interventions against.
Example: Digital transformation, operational efficiency, market expansion, leadership pipeline strengthening
🔢Total_Employees
InputNumeric InputNumber
Total number of employees currently in the organization.
Use the latest approved workforce count across all active employment categories. This is a direct count, not a band.
Example: 1200
📝Headquarters_Address
DependentText InputText
Full primary headquarters address of the organization.
Capture the complete official head-office address in one field for reference, instead of only city and country.
Example: No. 25, Innovation Park, Whitefield, Bengaluru, Karnataka 560066, India
📝Other_Business_Units_and_Locations
InputText InputText
Summary of major business units and key operating locations outside the headquarters.
Use this field to capture the broad organizational footprint across business units and major operating sites. It can be stored as a concise structured summary.
Example: Business Units: Consumer Products, Enterprise Services, Digital Solutions, Operations; Locations: Mumbai, Chennai, Hyderabad, Pune, Delhi, Singapore
🔢Countries_of_Operation
InputNumeric InputNumber
Countries in which the organization currently operates or serves customers through direct presence.
Use a comma-separated list or a controlled multi-value format based on the organization’s active operating footprint.
Example: India, UAE, Singapore
📅Last_Updated
InputDate PickerDate
Date on which this organization master record was last reviewed or updated.
Use a proper date field in YYYY-MM-DD or your standard enterprise date format.
Example: 2026-03-09
📆year
InputYear PickerYear
Historical year within the last 5-year organizational context series.
Use one row for each of the last 5 completed years to capture business and external-context signals affecting capability planning. This is a rolling 5-year history, not a single reporting-year field.
Example: 2021
📝market_conditions
InputText InputText
Overall market demand and competitive intensity in the reporting year.
Summarize the commercial environment such as stable, volatile, growing, or highly competitive.
Example: Moderate growth with rising private-label competition
📝macro_economic_conditions
InputText InputText
Macroeconomic factors influencing the organization during the year.
Capture inflation, interest rates, consumer spending, commodity costs, and other economy-wide effects.
Example: High inflation and increased input-cost pressure
📝geopolitical_events
InputText InputText
Major geopolitical developments affecting the organization or its supply chain.
Record wars, sanctions, border issues, trade restrictions, or regional instability where relevant.
Example: Global shipping disruptions due to regional conflict
📝technology_changes
InputText InputText
Important technology shifts impacting operations, products, or workforce capability needs.
Mention automation, AI adoption, system upgrades, platform changes, or production-tech changes.
Example: Expansion of AI-driven demand forecasting and plant automation
📝government_policy_changes
InputText InputText
Government policy or policy changes that materially affect the organization.
Include tax changes, subsidies, labor laws, food regulations, or trade-policy shifts.
Example: New food-labeling rules and stricter labor compliance norms
📝industry_disruptions
InputText InputText
Industry-specific disruptions observed in the year.
Capture category-specific shocks such as raw material scarcity, channel shifts, or competitor consolidation.
Example: Milk procurement volatility and rapid growth in value-added dairy
📝talent_market_conditions
InputText InputText
Availability, cost, and competitiveness of talent in the labor market.
Describe hiring difficulty, wage pressure, attrition environment, and niche skill shortages.
Example: High demand for automation technicians and cold-chain specialists
📝regulatory_environment
InputText InputText
Overall regulatory intensity and compliance climate for the year.
Use a concise description of whether compliance pressure is low, moderate, or high and why.
Example: High scrutiny on food safety, traceability, and ESG disclosures
📝sustainability_pressure
InputText InputText
Extent of sustainability-related pressure from regulators, customers, and investors.
Mention carbon, waste, packaging, water use, or responsible sourcing pressure.
Example: Rising pressure to cut packaging waste and plant emissions
📝digital_transformation_stage
InputDropdownText
Current maturity stage of digital transformation in the organization.
Use a controlled maturity scale such as Nascent, Emerging, Scaling, Mature, or Optimized.
Example: Scaling
📝key_strategic_priorities
InputText InputText
Top strategic priorities guiding the business during the year.
List the major priorities that should influence workforce and skill planning.
Example: Expand value-added dairy portfolio; improve plant efficiency; strengthen distributor analytics
📝workforce_challenges
InputText InputText
Key workforce capability or people-related issues faced during the year.
Describe shortages, engagement issues, succession risks, or reskilling needs.
Example: Shortage of maintenance engineers and first-line supervisors
📝supply_chain_environment
InputText InputText
State of the supply chain environment affecting delivery and operational reliability.
Include vendor reliability, cold-chain stability, logistics constraints, and sourcing risks.
Example: Moderate instability due to fuel-cost spikes and seasonal sourcing variation
📝carbon_emissions_tonnes
InputText InputText
Total carbon emissions for the reporting year, measured in tonnes.
Store the annual emissions value using a consistent methodology and unit definition.
Example: 18450
📆year
InputYear PickerYear
Historical year within the last 5-year organizational performance series.
Use one row for each of the last 5 completed years so trend analysis can be performed across financial, operational, and workforce metrics. This is a rolling 5-year history, not a single reporting-year field.
Example: 2021
💰total_sales_USD_million
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Total annual sales revenue in USD millions.
Store the audited or management-reported annual sales figure in millions of USD.
Example: 286.4
🔢location_count
InputNumeric InputNumber
Number of active operating locations in the reporting year.
Include plants, distribution centers, offices, or stores based on the agreed business rule.
Example: 42
🔢employee_count
InputNumeric InputNumber
Total number of active employees during the reporting year.
Use year-end active headcount or the defined annual headcount rule consistently.
Example: 1260
📊same_location_sales_growth_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Year-over-year sales growth percentage from comparable locations.
Use only locations operating in both comparison periods; store as a percentage.
Example: 6.8%
📊ecommerce_sales_share_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of total sales generated through ecommerce channels.
Store the share of total annual sales attributable to digital or ecommerce channels.
Example: 12.5%
📝total_milk_volume_processed_gallons_M
InputText InputText
Total milk volume processed in the year, measured in millions of gallons.
Use the annual operational total and keep the unit consistent as millions.
Example: 94.2
🔢product_SKU_count
InputNumeric InputNumber
Number of active product SKUs in the reporting year.
Count only active sellable SKUs as per the business catalog definition.
Example: 385
🔢retail_accounts_served
InputNumeric InputNumber
Number of retail customer accounts actively served in the year.
Count distinct active retail accounts receiving products or services.
Example: 1280
📊gross_margin_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Gross margin percentage for the reporting year.
Store as a percentage using the standard finance definition of gross profit divided by sales.
Example: 28.4%
📊operating_margin_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Operating margin percentage for the reporting year.
Store as a percentage using operating profit divided by sales.
Example: 11.2%
💰capex_USD_million
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Capital expenditure during the reporting year in USD millions.
Include approved and recorded capital investments for the year.
Example: 18.6
💰training_investment_USD
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Total annual spend on workforce training and capability development in USD.
Include internal and external learning spend based on the agreed accounting scope.
Example: 425000
🔑Organization_Certification_Record_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for each organization certification record.
Use a stable, non-reusable ID for each organization-level certification or accreditation entry.
Example: OCERT-0001
🔑Organization_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier of the certified organization.
Links the certification to the organization master and supports enterprise-level reporting.
Example: ORG-001
📝Organization_Name
DependentText InputText
Readable name of the certified organization or legal entity.
Human-readable reference for reporting, audit documentation, and governance review.
Example: Acme Global Services Pvt Ltd
📝Certification_Name
InputText InputText
Name of the certification or accreditation.
Capture the formal certification title granted to the organization, site, or legal entity.
Example: ISO 27001
📝Certification_Category
InputDropdownText
Broad category of the certification.
Use a standard category such as Information Security, Quality, Environmental, Safety, or Employer Recognition.
Example: Information Security
📝Issuing_Body
InputText InputText
Certifying authority or accrediting body.
Record the external organization that granted or oversees the certification.
Example: BSI
📝Certification_Scope
InputText InputText
Scope covered by the certification.
Describe what functions, services, sites, or processes are included within the certification boundary.
Example: Information security management for corporate operations and client delivery
📝Applicable_Legal_Entity
InputText InputText
Legal entity covered by the certification, if applicable.
Use when the certification applies only to a specific legal entity within a larger organization.
Example: Acme Global Services Pvt Ltd
📝Applicable_Business_Unit
InputText InputText
Business unit covered by the certification, if applicable.
Helps define certification coverage more precisely when it is not enterprise-wide.
Example: Technology Services
🔑Applicable_Location_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Location or site covered by the certification, if applicable.
Important for site-specific certifications, plant certifications, or delivery-center accreditations.
Example: LOC-002
📅Issue_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Date on which the certification was granted.
Used to track tenure of certification and support renewal/audit planning.
Example: 2023-05-10
📅Expiry_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Date on which the certification expires.
Supports validity monitoring and renewal readiness.
Example: 2026-05-09
📝Renewal_CycleRequired
InputDropdownText
Frequency of renewal or recertification.
Use values such as Annual, 2 Years, 3 Years, or As Required based on the certification rules.
Example: 3 Years
📝Surveillance_Audit_Frequency
InputText InputText
Frequency of surveillance or maintenance audits.
Important for compliance planning where the certification requires periodic external surveillance.
Example: Annual
📝Certification_Status
InputDropdownText
Current status of the certification.
Use controlled values such as Active, Under Renewal, Expired, Suspended, or Withdrawn.
Example: Active
📝Compliance_Relevance
InputText InputText
Level of compliance or regulatory relevance of the certification.
Indicates whether the certification meaningfully supports regulatory, contractual, or governance obligations.
Example: High
📝Strategic_Importance
InputDropdownText
Strategic value of the certification to the organization.
Use values such as Operational, Important, or Strategic depending on business dependence and market impact.
Example: Strategic
🔑Linked_Compliance_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Compliance requirement linked to the certification.
Connect the certification to the relevant requirement in Compliance_Master where appropriate.
Example: COMPREQ-004
📝Audit_Owner
InputText InputText
Person or function responsible for certification governance.
Tracks accountability for renewal, audit readiness, and certification maintenance.
Example: Compliance Manager
📅Last_Audit_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Most recent audit date related to the certification.
Useful for recency checks and governance tracking.
Example: 2025-04-15
📅Next_Audit_Due_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Next scheduled audit due date.
Helps with governance planning and certification continuity management.
Example: 2026-04-15
📝Certification_Benefit_Area
InputText InputText
Main business value delivered by the certification.
Summarizes why the certification matters, such as client trust, regulatory readiness, or operational quality.
Example: Client trust and regulatory readiness
📄Remarks
InputTextareaLongtext
Additional notes or contextual comments.
Use for scope expansion, pending renewal, exceptions, or audit observations.
Example: Scope expansion planned for two additional locations

Location Management

2 tabs · 21 fields
🔑Hierarchy_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for each hierarchy mapping record.
Use one unique row ID for every hierarchy assignment so lineage and updates can be tracked without ambiguity.
Example: LH-0001
📝Zone
InputDropdownText
Broadest geographic or operating zone to which the location belongs.
Use a standardized zone structure such as North, South, East, West, Central, or another approved enterprise geography model.
Example: South Zone
📝Region
InputText InputText
Regional grouping within the zone.
Capture the formal business region used for operations, reporting, or workforce planning.
Example: Karnataka Region
📝Cluster
InputText InputText
Smaller grouping of nearby or similar locations within a region.
Use when the organization manages multiple locations in sub-regional clusters for staffing, performance, or administration.
Example: Bengaluru Urban Cluster
🔑Location_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier of the location represented in this hierarchy row.
Must match an existing Location_ID in the Location_Master sheet. Do not use free-text location names as keys.
Example: LOC-0104
📝Location_Name
DependentText InputText
Official name of the location linked to the hierarchy.
Use the approved operational name of the site, branch, office, plant, warehouse, or store equivalent.
Example: Indiranagar Distribution Center
📝Location_Type
InputDropdownText
Category of location in the enterprise operating model.
Use a controlled list such as Office, Plant, Branch, Warehouse, Store, Lab, Campus, Delivery Center, or Factory.
Example: Warehouse
📝Tier
InputDropdownText
Relative classification level of the location for business scale, complexity, or strategic importance.
Define tiers through a standard enterprise rule such as Tier 1 for strategic or large locations, Tier 2 for regional, and Tier 3 for smaller sites.
Example: Tier 2
🔑Parent_Location_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Identifier of the parent or reporting location in the hierarchy.
Use this when a location reports into another location node. Leave blank only if the location is the top node in that hierarchy branch.
Example: LOC-0001
📄Hierarchy_Level_Notes
InputTextareaLongtext
Optional notes describing hierarchy rules, exceptions, or structural logic.
Use this field to capture unusual reporting relationships or enterprise-specific hierarchy interpretation notes.
Example: This location reports to the regional warehouse hub for staffing and logistics oversight.
🔑location_idRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier assigned to each physical or operating location.
Must be unique, stable, and reusable across systems for integration with employee, performance, and hierarchy data.
Example: LOC-0104
📝location_name
InputText InputText
Official operational name of the location.
Use the approved site name so reporting remains consistent across HR, operations, and skills data.
Example: Indiranagar Distribution Center
📝city
InputText InputText
City in which the location operates.
Capture the actual operating city of the location, not the headquarters city unless both are the same.
Example: Bengaluru
📝state
InputDropdownText
State, province, or administrative region where the location is situated.
Use the standard state or province name used in enterprise records.
Example: Karnataka
📝region
InputText InputText
Business or administrative region assigned to the location.
This should align with the enterprise regional structure used for operational reporting.
Example: South Region
📝zone
InputText InputText
Higher-level zone classification assigned to the location.
Use the approved top-level geography or operating zone classification.
Example: South Zone
📝location_type
InputDropdownText
Type of enterprise location represented in the record.
Use a controlled list such as Office, Plant, Branch, Warehouse, Store, Factory, Campus, or Delivery Center.
Example: Warehouse
📝location_size_sqft
InputText InputText
Approximate built-up or usable size of the location in square feet.
Enter a numeric value only. Use the most recent validated facility size for capacity and workforce planning.
Example: 25000
📆opening_year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the location became operational.
Use the year the facility, site, or location started formal operations. For historical sites, capture the original go-live year where available.
Example: 2018
📝status
InputDropdownText
Current operational status of the location.
Use controlled values such as Active, Inactive, Planned, Closed, or Under Renovation depending on enterprise governance.
Example: Active
Dropdown: Active, Inactive, Planned, Retired
📝manager_or_owner
InputText InputText
Primary accountable owner or managing leader for the location.
Capture the designated manager, facility owner, or accountable business lead responsible for the location.
Example: Rohit Sharma

Competitor Benchmarking

1 tabs · 80 fields
📝Category
InputText InputText
High-level benchmark theme or capability area being compared.
Use broad categories such as Financial, Workforce, Skills, Operations, Quality, Customer, Digital, Compliance, ESG, or Innovation so peer comparisons can be grouped consistently.
Example: Workforce
📝Parameter
InputText InputText
Specific benchmark metric or comparison attribute under the category.
Use one measurable item per row, such as attrition rate, revenue per employee, on-time delivery, automation coverage, training hours, or certification coverage.
Example: Annual Attrition Rate %
📝Industry_Benchmark
DependentText InputText
Market, sector, or peer-group reference point used for comparison.
Enter a benchmark range, average, percentile, ratio, or target value from the relevant industry, geography, and company size segment.
Example: 14%-18%
📝Company_Under_Review
InputDropdownText
Performance or value for the organization currently being assessed.
Use the actual value of the focal company being benchmarked against peers and industry standards.
Example: 16.2%
📝Company_A
InputText InputText
Benchmark value for anonymized peer company A.
Use this for a comparable company of similar scale, market, and operating model without revealing its actual name.
Example: 15.4%
📝Company_B
InputText InputText
Benchmark value for anonymized peer company B.
Use this for a second anonymized peer comparator selected using the same peer-selection logic.
Example: 13.8%
📝Company_C
InputText InputText
Benchmark value for anonymized peer company C.
Use this for a third anonymized peer comparator to support broader market context while preserving confidentiality.
Example: 17.1%
📝Suggested Same-Industry Benchmark Parameters
InputText InputText
Section header row listing suggested same-industry benchmark parameters organized by category.
Example: Financial, Workforce, Skills & Capability, Operations
📝Benchmark_Category
InputText InputText
Suggested_Parameter
Definition / What to Compare
Example: Example_Benchmark_or_Unit
📝Financial
InputText InputText
Revenue Growth %
Year-over-year revenue growth performance.
Example: 8.5%
📝Financial
InputText InputText
EBITDA Margin %
Operating profitability margin before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization.
Example: 12.0%
📝Financial
InputText InputText
Gross Margin %
Gross profit margin relative to revenue.
Example: 28.0%
📝Financial
InputText InputText
Net Profit Margin %
Net income as a percentage of revenue.
Example: 6.5%
📝Financial
InputText InputText
Revenue per Employee
Revenue generated per full-time equivalent.
Example: $185,000
📝Financial
InputText InputText
Cost per Unit Produced
Average production or service delivery cost per unit.
Example: $2.80 per liter
📝Financial
InputText InputText
Training Investment as % of Payroll
Learning spend relative to total payroll cost.
Example: 1.8%
📝Financial
InputText InputText
Capex as % of Revenue
Capital expenditure intensity versus revenue.
Example: 4.2%
📝Workforce
InputText InputText
Total Headcount
Total number of employees in scope.
Example: 2,450
📝Workforce
InputText InputText
Annual Attrition Rate %
Percentage of employees leaving in a year.
Example: 16.2%
📝Workforce
InputText InputText
Critical Talent Attrition %
Attrition within high-impact or hard-to-replace roles.
Example: 8.0%
📝Workforce
InputText InputText
Absenteeism Rate %
Share of lost time due to absence.
Example: 3.2%
📝WorkforceRequired
InputText InputText
Time to Fill Roles
Average days required to close open positions.
Example: 42 days
📝Workforce
InputText InputText
Offer Acceptance Rate %
Share of accepted offers out of total offers made.
Example: 78%
📝Workforce
InputText InputText
Internal Mobility Rate %
Share of vacancies filled internally.
Example: 24%
📝Workforce
InputText InputText
Span of Control
Average number of direct reports per manager.
Example: 1:8
📝Workforce
InputText InputText
Gender Diversity %
Representation of women or diverse gender groups in workforce or leadership.
Example: 34%
📝Workforce
InputText InputText
Overtime Hours per FTE
Average overtime hours per employee.
Example: 11 hrs/month
📝Skills & CapabilityRequired
InputText InputText
Critical Skill Coverage %
Share of critical skills meeting required proficiency.
Example: 72%
📝Skills & Capability
InputText InputText
Average Skill Proficiency Score
Average current proficiency across prioritized skills.
Example: 3.4 / 5
📝Skills & Capability
InputText InputText
Skill Gap Severity Index
Composite measure of depth and spread of skill gaps.
Example: 0.38
📝Skills & Capability
InputText InputText
Role Readiness %
Share of employees meeting role skill thresholds.
Example: 68%
📝Skills & CapabilityRequired
InputText InputText
Certification Coverage %
Share of target workforce with required certifications.
Example: 61%
📝Skills & Capability
InputText InputText
Future Skill Readiness %
Coverage level for emerging or strategic future skills.
Example: 44%
📝Skills & Capability
InputText InputText
Competency Maturity Score
Average behavioral/managerial competency maturity.
Example: 3.2 / 5
📝Learning & Development
InputText InputText
Training Hours per Employee
Average formal learning hours per employee annually.
Example: 22 hrs
📝Learning & Development
InputText InputText
Learning Completion Rate %
Share of enrolled learning completed.
Example: 87%
📝Learning & DevelopmentRequired
InputText InputText
Mandatory Training Compliance %
Completion rate for mandatory learning.
Example: 96%
📝Learning & Development
InputText InputText
Post-Training Assessment Gain %
Average lift between pre- and post-training assessments.
Example: 18%
📝Learning & Development
InputText InputText
Training Effectiveness Score
Average training reaction / effectiveness index.
Example: 4.3 / 5
📝Learning & Development
InputText InputText
Learning Application Rate %
Share of learners applying skills on the job after training.
Example: 63%
📝Learning & Development
InputText InputText
Learning Cost per Learner
Average cost incurred per trained employee.
Example: $420
📝Operations
InputText InputText
Capacity Utilization %
Actual output relative to available capacity.
Example: 81%
📝Operations
InputText InputText
On-Time Delivery %
Orders or services delivered within promised time.
Example: 94%
📝Operations
InputText InputText
Production Yield %
Usable output as a share of total produced output.
Example: 97.5%
📝Operations
InputText InputText
Downtime %
Share of scheduled time lost to equipment or process stoppage.
Example: 4.6%
📝Operations
InputText InputText
Inventory Turnover
Rate at which inventory is sold and replaced.
Example: 9.2x
📝Operations
InputText InputText
Forecast Accuracy %
Accuracy of demand or production forecast.
Example: 86%
📝Operations
InputText InputText
Order Fulfillment Cycle Time
Average time from order receipt to completion.
Example: 2.8 days
📝Quality
InputDropdownText
Defect Rate %
Share of output not meeting quality standards.
Example: 1.2%
📝Quality
InputText InputText
First Pass Yield %
Percentage of units passing without rework.
Example: 96.4%
📝Quality
InputText InputText
Customer Complaint Rate
Number of complaints per thousand units/orders.
Example: 2.1 per 1,000
📝Quality
InputText InputText
Recall / Incident Frequency
Frequency of quality recalls or major incidents.
Example: 0-1 annually
📝Quality
InputText InputText
Audit Non-Conformance Count
Number of audit findings or quality non-conformances.
Example: 6
📝Customer & Market
InputText InputText
Market Share %
Company share within relevant market or category.
Example: 11.5%
📝Customer & Market
InputText InputText
Net Promoter Score
Customer willingness to recommend.
Example: 48
📝Customer & Market
InputText InputText
Customer Retention %
Rate of repeat or retained customers.
Example: 89%
📝Customer & Market
InputText InputText
Average Selling Price
Average realized price per unit/service.
Example: $3.10
📝Customer & Market
InputText InputText
New Product Revenue %
Revenue contribution from recently launched products.
Example: 14%
📝Digital & Technology
InputText InputText
Automation Coverage %
Share of core processes automated.
Example: 39%
📝Digital & Technology
InputText InputText
Digital Adoption Rate %
Active usage of target digital tools/platforms.
Example: 74%
📝Digital & Technology
InputText InputText
Data Quality Score
Composite quality score for critical enterprise data.
Example: 92 / 100
📝Digital & Technology
InputText InputText
System Downtime Hours
Annual downtime in key platforms.
Example: 14 hrs
📝Digital & Technology
InputText InputText
AI / Analytics Utilization %
Share of priority decisions or workflows using analytics/AI.
Example: 27%
📝Compliance & Risk
InputText InputText
Regulatory Compliance Score
Overall compliance adherence across audits/controls.
Example: 95%
📝Compliance & Risk
InputText InputText
Safety Incident Rate
Recordable incidents per 100 or 200 employees/FTEs.
Example: 1.8 TRIR
📝Compliance & Risk
InputText InputText
Food / Product Safety Violations
Count of safety or regulatory violations.
Example: 0
📝Compliance & Risk
InputText InputText
Risk Mitigation Closure %
Share of identified risk actions closed on time.
Example: 84%
📝Compliance & Risk
InputText InputText
Cybersecurity Training Compliance %
Completion rate for cyber awareness training.
Example: 98%
📝Supply Chain
InputText InputText
Supplier OTIF %
On-time in-full supplier delivery performance.
Example: 91%
📝Supply Chain
InputText InputText
Procurement Savings %
Savings achieved through sourcing/procurement initiatives.
Example: 3.6%
📝Supply Chain
InputText InputText
Cold Chain / Storage Compliance %
Adherence to storage or temperature requirements where relevant.
Example: 99.2%
📝Supply Chain
InputText InputText
Lead Time Variability
Variation in supplier or replenishment lead times.
Example: ±1.4 days
📝ESG & Sustainability
InputText InputText
Energy Consumption per Unit
Energy intensity per production/service unit.
Example: 0.85 kWh/liter
📝ESG & Sustainability
InputText InputText
Water Usage per Unit
Water consumption intensity per unit output.
Example: 2.1 liters/liter
📝ESG & Sustainability
InputText InputText
Waste Recovery / Recycling %
Share of waste recovered or recycled.
Example: 76%
📝ESG & Sustainability
InputText InputText
Carbon Emissions Intensity
Emissions per unit of output or revenue.
Example: 0.42 kg CO2e/unit
📝Innovation
InputText InputText
R&D / Innovation Spend % of Revenue
Investment in innovation relative to revenue.
Example: 2.4%
📝Innovation
InputText InputText
New Product Launch Success Rate %
Share of launches meeting target outcomes.
Example: 71%
📝Innovation
InputText InputText
Process Improvement Savings
Annual savings from process improvement programs.
Example: $1.2M
📝Innovation
InputText InputText
Idea-to-Implementation Cycle Time
Average time from validated idea to implementation.
Example: 73 days

Portfolio Management

5 tabs · 51 fields
🔑Product_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for each product offering in the platform.
Use a stable, non-reusable code for every product, SKU family, or catalog item tracked for revenue, capability mapping, or workforce demand analysis.
Example: PROD-001
📝Product_Name
InputText InputText
Business name of the product offered by the organization.
Use the official commercial or internal catalog name that business, finance, operations, and sales teams recognize consistently.
Example: Premium Subscription Plan
📝Product_Category
InputDropdownText
Broad classification used to group similar products.
Use a controlled list such as Hardware, Software, Consumer Goods, Financial Product, Learning Product, Medical Device, or Packaged Product depending on the business model.
Example: Software Product
📝Product_Type
InputDropdownText
More specific type or format of the product within its category.
Use a finer classification such as SaaS, Mobile App, Device, Consumable, Policy, Course Bundle, or Physical Equipment so capability requirements can be segmented properly.
Example: SaaS Platform
📝Business_Unit
InputText InputText
Business unit or line of business that owns or manages the product.
Capture the owning unit responsible for product strategy, delivery, profitability, and talent planning.
Example: Enterprise Solutions
📆Launch_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the product was first launched to the market or internally released.
Use the original launch year for lifecycle analysis; if relaunched, maintain the original product launch year and manage versions separately.
Example: 2022
📝Lifecycle_Stage
InputDropdownText
Current stage of the product in its business lifecycle.
Use a controlled list such as Concept, Development, Pilot, Launch, Growth, Mature, Declining, Sunset, or Retired.
Example: Growth
📝Unit_of_Measure
InputText InputText
Commercial, service, or inventory unit used to count or sell the product.
Use a generic unit such as License, Unit, Subscription, Seat, Device, Kit, Box, Policy, User, or Course Access instead of industry-specific packaging terms.
Example: Subscription
📝Delivery_Channel
InputDropdownText
Primary channel through which the product reaches the customer or end user.
Use a controlled list such as Direct Sales, Distributor, Retail, E-commerce, Channel Partner, Marketplace, Mobile App, or Internal Delivery.
Example: Direct Sales
📝Primary_Market
InputText InputText
Main customer market, geography, or segment targeted by the product.
Capture the dominant market orientation such as B2B, B2C, Enterprise, SME, Healthcare, Public Sector, Domestic, or Global.
Example: Enterprise B2B
📊Revenue_Contribution_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Share of total organization revenue contributed by the product.
Use a percentage of total revenue for the reporting period; this helps prioritize critical products and associated skill investments.
Example: 12.5%
📝Regulatory_Criticality
InputDropdownText
Level of regulatory, compliance, or quality sensitivity associated with the product.
Use a controlled scale such as Low, Medium, High, or Critical based on the impact of compliance failure or regulatory scrutiny.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
✅Active_Status
InputDropdownBoolean
Indicates whether the product is currently active in the portfolio.
Use Active, Inactive, Planned, Pilot, Retired, or Archived so downstream analytics can filter relevant products correctly.
Example: Active
Dropdown: Active, Inactive, Planned, Retired
🔑Service_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for each service offering tracked in the platform.
Use a stable, non-reusable ID for every managed service, consulting offer, support line, subscription service, or internal service catalog item.
Example: SRV-001
📝Service_Name
InputText InputText
Business name of the service offering.
Use the official service name recognized across sales, operations, delivery, finance, and workforce planning functions.
Example: Managed Analytics Service
📝Service_Type
InputDropdownText
Primary format or operating model of the service.
Use a controlled list such as Consulting, Managed Service, Support Service, Implementation, Training Service, Subscription Service, Shared Service, or Field Service.
Example: Managed Service
📝Service_Category
InputDropdownText
Broad service grouping used for reporting and portfolio segmentation.
Use categories such as Customer Support, Professional Services, Maintenance, Learning Services, Technology Services, Advisory, or Operations Support.
Example: Technology Services
📝Business_Unit
InputText InputText
Business unit or line of business that owns the service.
Capture the unit accountable for service design, commercial performance, delivery quality, and workforce capability.
Example: Digital Operations
📆Start_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the service was first introduced or commercially launched.
Use the original introduction year to support service maturity and lifecycle analysis.
Example: 2021
📝Delivery_Model
InputDropdownText
Primary mode through which the service is delivered to customers or internal stakeholders.
Use a controlled list such as Onsite, Remote, Hybrid, Self-Service, Assisted Service, Shared Service Center, or Managed Delivery.
Example: Hybrid
📝Primary_Customer_Segment
InputText InputText
Main customer segment or business audience served by the service.
Use labels such as Enterprise, SME, Consumer, Internal Employees, Healthcare Providers, Public Sector, or Channel Partners.
Example: Enterprise
📄Service_Description
InputTextareaLongtext
Short description of what the service delivers and the business need it addresses.
Write a concise, business-readable summary of the scope, outcomes, and target users of the service.
Example: Provides ongoing dashboard management, KPI monitoring, and advanced analytics support for enterprise clients.
📝Service_Level_Tier
InputDropdownText
Tier or level of service commitment associated with the offering.
Use Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum, Standard, Premium, Basic, or a similar controlled scale if service levels differ by coverage or response commitments.
Example: Premium
📊Revenue_Contribution_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Share of total organization revenue contributed by the service.
Use the percentage contribution for the reporting period to support service prioritization and capability planning.
Example: 18.0%
📝Critical_Skills_DependenceRequired
InputDropdownText
Level of dependence on scarce or high-impact skills required to deliver the service.
Use Low, Medium, High, or Critical to indicate how much service delivery depends on specialized capability availability.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝Primary_Location_or_Channel
InputText InputText
Main location, delivery center, or channel from which the service is delivered.
Use a generic operating descriptor such as Chennai Delivery Center, Remote Delivery Hub, Field Network, Shared Service Center, or Digital Platform.
Example: Remote Delivery Hub
✅Active_Status
InputDropdownBoolean
Indicates whether the service is currently active in the service portfolio.
Use Active, Inactive, Planned, Pilot, Retired, or Archived for filtering and lifecycle reporting.
Example: Active
Dropdown: Active, Inactive, Planned, Retired
🔑Product_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier linking the skill requirement row to a product in the product portfolio.
Use the same ID as in Product_Portfolio so product capability requirements can be joined reliably across sheets.
Example: PROD-001
📝Product_Name
InputText InputText
Readable business name of the product associated with the skill requirement.
Keep the official product name for reporting clarity; the ID remains the system join key.
Example: Premium Subscription Plan
📝Required_Skill
InputText InputText
Specific skill needed to design, deliver, support, maintain, or sell the product effectively.
Capture one skill per row so the platform can map product complexity to workforce capability and training demand.
Example: Cloud Architecture
📝Skill_LevelRequired
InputDropdownText
Required proficiency level for the mapped skill in the context of that product.
Use a controlled scale such as Beginner, Intermediate, Advanced, Expert, or a numeric proficiency framework approved in the platform.
Example: Advanced
📝Criticality
InputDropdownText
Importance of the skill to product success, continuity, quality, or compliance.
Use a business priority label such as Low, Medium, High, or Critical to support talent prioritization and risk analysis.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝Skill_CategoryRequired
InputDropdownText
Broader grouping of the required skill for reporting and capability planning.
Use categories such as Technical, Functional, Digital, Sales, Customer Service, Compliance, Leadership, or Domain Knowledge.
Example: Technical
📄Notes
InputTextareaLongtext
Optional context explaining why the skill is mapped or how it is applied to the product.
Use this to capture implementation caveats, specialization needs, certification expectations, or role-specific comments.
Example: Needed for secure deployment and environment scaling support.
🔑Service_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier linking the skill requirement row to a service in the service portfolio.
Use the same ID as in Service_Portfolio so service capability requirements can be mapped consistently.
Example: SRV-001
📝Service_Name
InputText InputText
Readable business name of the service associated with the skill requirement.
Retain the official service name for reporting and business interpretation; use the ID as the main join key.
Example: Managed Analytics Service
📝Required_Skill
InputText InputText
Specific skill needed to deliver, support, govern, or improve the service.
Capture one skill per row to enable clear mapping between service offerings and workforce capability needs.
Example: Stakeholder Management
📝Skill_LevelRequired
InputText InputText
Required proficiency level for the mapped skill in the context of that service.
Use an approved proficiency scale so service-readiness analysis is consistent across offerings.
Example: Intermediate
📝Criticality
InputDropdownText
Business importance of the skill for service quality, continuity, customer experience, or compliance.
Use labels such as Low, Medium, High, or Critical to prioritize hiring, training, and succession planning.
Example: Critical
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📄Notes
InputTextareaLongtext
Optional explanatory field providing service-specific context for the skill requirement.
Record special delivery constraints, regulatory expectations, service-level implications, or certification requirements here.
Example: Essential for escalation handling in premium support engagements.
🔑Project_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for each project tracked in the platform.
Use a stable, non-reusable ID for every strategic, operational, transformation, compliance, customer, or internal improvement project.
Example: PRJ-001
📝Project_Name
InputText InputText
Business name of the project or initiative.
Use the official project title recognized by PMO, delivery, finance, and leadership teams.
Example: Enterprise CRM Modernization
📝Location_or_Unit
DependentText InputText
Primary location, site, branch, delivery center, or business unit associated with the project.
Use a generic organizational reference instead of industry-specific site names so the model works across sectors.
Example: Bengaluru Delivery Center
📆Start_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the project started or is planned to start.
Capture the actual or approved start year to support project load, demand forecasting, and capability planning over time.
Example: 2024
📆End_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the project ended or is expected to end.
Use the planned or actual end year; if the project is ongoing, update as forecasts change.
Example: 2026
📝Primary_Skill
InputText InputText
Main skill area most critical to project execution or success.
Use the dominant skill dependency when one capability is central; detailed many-to-many skill mapping can be maintained in other sheets if needed.
Example: Program Management
📝Project_Type
InputDropdownText
Broad type or purpose of the project.
Use a controlled list such as Transformation, Compliance, Product Development, Cost Optimization, Customer Delivery, Technology Upgrade, or Internal Improvement.
Example: Transformation
💰Budget_USD
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Approved, planned, or current budget value associated with the project in USD.
Store the total project budget in a single standard currency for comparison, portfolio review, and workforce investment prioritization.
Example: 2500000
📝Project_Status
Renamed from Status
InputDropdownText
Current execution state of the project.
Use a standard set such as Planned, Approved, Active, On Hold, Delayed, Completed, or Cancelled.
Example: Active
📝Business_Outcome
InputText InputText
Expected or realized business value the project is intended to deliver.
Describe the main outcome in business terms such as revenue growth, cost reduction, risk mitigation, customer experience improvement, or capability expansion.
Example: Improved customer retention and faster cross-team service delivery.
🔑Lead_Employee_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Identifier of the primary accountable project lead or manager.
Use the employee ID of the project owner, delivery lead, program manager, or accountable sponsor representative as defined in governance rules.
Example: EMP-104

Technology Stack

1 tabs · 33 fields
🔑Tech_Record_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for each technology history record.
Use a stable, non-reusable ID for each year-wise technology or tool entry captured in the organization.
Example: TECH-0001
📆Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the technology or tool status is being recorded.
Capture one row per technology/tool per year across the last 5 years to preserve historical technology-stack evolution.
Example: 2025
📝Business_Unit
InputText InputText
Business unit primarily using or owning the technology/tool.
Use the relevant business unit, function, or enterprise service group responsible for the technology’s usage or governance.
Example: Operations
📝Department_or_Function
InputText InputText
Department or function most closely associated with the technology/tool.
Helps connect the technology/tool to the operating area, workflow, and impacted role groups.
Example: Supply Chain
📝Technology_Category
InputDropdownText
Broad category of the technology or tool.
Classify the tool into a major category such as ERP, CRM, HRMS, LMS, Collaboration, Data & Analytics, Cybersecurity, or Automation.
Example: ERP
📝Technology_Subcategory
InputText InputText
More specific classification within the broader technology category.
Use a more precise subcategory to show what type of solution it is within the main category.
Example: Inventory Management System
📝Technology_or_Tool_Name
InputText InputText
Name of the technology platform, software tool, or system.
Capture the actual product, platform, or internally developed system name used by the organization.
Example: SAP S/4HANA
📝Vendor_or_Provider
InputText InputText
Vendor, provider, or internal owner of the technology/tool.
Use the external vendor name or internal platform owner responsible for the tool.
Example: SAP
📝Tool_Type
InputText InputText
Type of solution being used.
Distinguish whether the item is a platform, application, suite, utility, system, portal, or custom tool.
Example: Enterprise Platform
📝Deployment_Model
InputText InputText
Hosting or deployment model of the technology/tool.
Useful for digital maturity, IT governance, and infrastructure planning across on-premise, cloud, or hybrid environments.
Example: Cloud
📝Usage_Status
InputDropdownText
Current status of the technology/tool for the given year.
Use controlled values such as Active, Pilot, Planned, Retired, Phased Out, or Upgraded.
Example: Active
📝Adoption_Level
InputDropdownText
Extent of adoption of the technology/tool in the organization.
Indicates whether usage is low, medium, high, or enterprise-wide during that year.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📆Introduced_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the technology/tool was first introduced in the organization.
Use the first year the tool became active, piloted, or officially launched in the organization.
Example: 2022
📆Retired_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the technology/tool was retired, if applicable.
Populate only if the technology/tool has been decommissioned or phased out.
Example: 2025
📝Primary_Use_Case
InputText InputText
Main business use case served by the technology/tool.
Describe the primary value or purpose for which the technology/tool is used.
Example: Enterprise resource planning and process integration
📝Business_Process_Supported
InputText InputText
Main business process supported by the technology/tool.
Use the core workflow or business process most directly enabled by the technology/tool.
Example: Procurement and inventory planning
📝User_Base_Type
InputText InputText
Primary type of users of the technology/tool.
Identify the main employee groups, personas, or user types using the technology/tool.
Example: Managers and Analysts
🔢Estimated_User_Count
InputNumeric InputNumber
Estimated number of users using the technology/tool.
Use a reasonable current or year-specific estimate of users actively using the tool.
Example: 180
📝Locations_Using_Tool
InputText InputText
Number or list of locations using the technology/tool.
Shows the geographic or organizational spread of the technology/tool across the enterprise.
Example: 8
🔢Countries_Using_Tool
InputNumeric InputNumber
Countries in which the technology/tool is used.
Capture the countries where the technology/tool is currently active or supported.
Example: India, UAE
📝Criticality_Level
InputDropdownText
Importance of the technology/tool to business continuity and operations.
Use controlled values such as Low, Moderate, High, or Mission Critical.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝Digital_Maturity_Contribution
InputText InputText
Extent to which the technology/tool contributes to digital maturity.
Indicates how strongly the technology/tool supports process digitization, analytics, automation, or digital transformation.
Example: Significant
📝Automation_Impact
InputText InputText
Extent to which the technology/tool enables workflow or process automation.
Helps assess future capability shifts and technology-driven role redesign.
Example: Medium
📝Roles_Impacted
InputText InputText
Roles significantly impacted by the technology/tool.
List the key job roles that require, depend on, or are materially influenced by the technology/tool.
Example: Supply Chain Analyst, Operations Manager
📝Skills_ImpactedRequired
InputText InputText
Skills affected, enabled, or required by the technology/tool.
Connect the technology/tool to the technical, digital, business, or process skills it influences.
Example: ERP Navigation, Data Analysis, Process Compliance
📝Competencies_Impacted
InputText InputText
Competencies influenced by the technology/tool.
Used to show which applied capabilities or behavioral competencies are strengthened or demanded by the tool.
Example: Decision Making, Cross-Functional Collaboration
✅Training_Required_FlagRequired
InputToggleBoolean
Indicates whether formal learning is needed to use the technology/tool.
Use Yes/No to show whether structured training is required for adoption or effective usage.
Example: Yes
Dropdown: Yes, No
📝Training_Program_Linked
DependentText InputText
Associated training program linked to the technology/tool, if any.
Reference the internal or external training program that supports capability building for the tool.
Example: ERP User Enablement Program
📝Skill_Transformation_Impact
InputText InputText
Description of how the technology/tool changes workforce capability requirements.
Explain how the tool alters skill demand, digital expectations, or work execution patterns over time.
Example: Increased need for digital workflow literacy and system-based decision making
📝Replacement_or_Upgrade_Details
InputText InputText
Details of any upgrade, migration, or replacement related to the technology/tool.
Capture major transitions such as product upgrades, platform migration, or legacy replacement over the last 5 years.
Example: Migrated from legacy on-prem ERP to SAP S/4HANA Cloud
📝Technology_Risk_or_Constraint
InputText InputText
Major risks, constraints, or limitations related to the technology/tool.
Document adoption barriers, integration risks, support constraints, or user challenges.
Example: Low user adoption in field teams
📝Strategic_Importance
InputText InputText
Strategic value of the technology/tool to the organization.
Classify the technology/tool as Operational, Tactical, or Strategic based on business significance.
Example: Strategic
📄Remarks
InputTextareaLongtext
Additional notes or contextual remarks.
Use for comments that do not fit any other field but help explain usage, rollout, or context.
Example: Planned expansion to two additional locations next year

Risk & Crisis

2 tabs · 38 fields
📆year
InputYear PickerYear
Calendar year for the last 5 completed years of supply-chain and operational risk tracking.
Use one row per completed year so trends in supply continuity, supplier resilience, delivery reliability, and procurement risk can be analyzed longitudinally.
Example: 2025
📝supply_chain_risk_level
InputDropdownText
Overall annual risk rating for supply-chain stability and continuity.
Use a normalized label or score such as Low, Medium, High, Critical, or a defined numeric risk scale approved by the organization.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝logistics_disruption_events
InputText InputText
Count of significant logistics or distribution disruptions recorded during the year.
Include major transport, customs, route, carrier, warehousing, or distribution interruptions that materially affected delivery performance.
Example: 14
📊vendor_regulation_compliance_%Required
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of key suppliers meeting required regulatory, contractual, or policy compliance standards.
Track the share of in-scope vendors that passed applicable compliance checks during the year.
Example: 92%
📝critical_input_supply_risk
InputText InputText
Risk level associated with the continuity, price stability, or availability of the organization’s most critical input materials or resources.
Use this generic field for whichever input category is most essential to business continuity, rather than naming an industry-specific raw material.
Example: Medium
📝key_material_risk
InputText InputText
Risk level associated with availability, price volatility, or quality consistency of important supporting materials or components.
Use this to capture risks tied to packaging, parts, consumables, modules, components, or any other essential supporting material.
Example: High
📝critical_storage_or_transit_failure_events
InputText InputText
Number of major storage, handling, or in-transit environment control failures during the year.
Use this for incidents where products, assets, or materials were damaged or compromised due to inadequate storage or transit conditions.
Example: 3
📝field_or_logistics_staff_shortage_severity
InputText InputText
Severity of labor shortage affecting field operations, transport, warehousing, delivery, or supply-chain execution.
Use a consistent rating scale to reflect workforce constraints that impaired operational continuity or increased lead times.
Example: Severe
💰fuel_cost_index
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Indexed measure of fuel or transportation-energy cost pressure during the year.
Use a standardized annual index to compare logistics cost pressure over time, even if the underlying energy mix differs across industries.
Example: 118.4
🔢critical_supplier_count
InputNumeric InputNumber
Number of active strategic or critical suppliers on which business continuity materially depends.
Count only suppliers whose failure, disruption, or exit would significantly affect operations, compliance, or customer commitments.
Example: 28
💰avg_critical_input_procurement_cost_usd
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Average procurement cost in USD for the organization’s most critical input category during the year.
Use a normalized annual average for the most important input, material, or resource to monitor inflation and sourcing pressure.
Example: 425.75
📊dual_source_coverage_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of critical input volume or spend covered by dual-source or alternate-source arrangements.
Higher values indicate better resilience because dependency on single-source supply is reduced.
Example: 64%
📊on_time_delivery_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of in-scope deliveries completed on or before the committed delivery date.
Measure this for key suppliers, logistics partners, or inbound supply commitments using a consistent service-level definition.
Example: 95%
📄key_risk_narrative
InputTextareaLongtext
Short annual narrative summarizing the most material supply-chain risks, causes, and mitigation themes.
Write a concise explanation of the main risk drivers, incidents, structural vulnerabilities, and major corrective actions for that year.
Example: Transport delays and single-source component dependency increased lead-time volatility; mitigation focused on alternate sourcing and regional warehousing.
🔑Crisis_Record_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for each crisis or disaster record.
Use a stable, non-reusable ID for each disruption, crisis, or disaster event entry.
Example: CRS-0001
📆Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the crisis or disaster occurred or was active.
Capture one row per event-year so major disruptions can be tracked historically across the last 5 years.
Example: 2024
📝Crisis_or_Disaster_Name
InputText InputText
Name of the crisis, disruption, or event.
Use a clear label for the event to support history, review, and scenario analysis.
Example: Regional Flood Disruption
📝Crisis_Category
InputDropdownText
Broad category of the crisis or disaster.
Classify events into categories such as Natural Disaster, Health Emergency, Cyber Incident, Political Disruption, Infrastructure Failure, or Supply Shock.
Example: Natural Disaster
📝Crisis_Subcategory
InputText InputText
More specific classification of the event.
Provides finer detail within the main crisis category for better analysis and planning.
Example: Flooding
📝Geographic_Area_Affected
InputText InputText
Location, region, cluster, or geography affected by the event.
Helps understand the geographic footprint and concentration of impact.
Example: South India Operations Cluster
📝Business_Unit_Affected
InputText InputText
Business unit affected by the event, if applicable.
Supports business-unit impact analysis and resilience planning.
Example: Operations
🔑Location_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Specific location affected by the event, if applicable.
Use when the event can be tied to a particular site or operating location.
Example: LOC-007
📅Start_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Date on which the crisis or disruption started.
Used to measure duration, operational response, and recovery cycle.
Example: 2024-09-05
📅End_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Date on which the crisis or disruption ended or stabilized.
Supports duration analysis and closure review.
Example: 2024-09-21
🔢Duration_Days
InputNumeric InputNumber
Total duration of the crisis or disruption in days.
Used to assess severity, continuity exposure, and recovery complexity.
Example: 16
📝Severity_Level
InputDropdownText
Severity of the crisis or disruption.
Use controlled values such as Low, Medium, High, or Critical based on business impact.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝Operational_Impact
InputText InputText
Description of the impact on operations.
Summarize how the event affected service delivery, facilities, production, or workflow continuity.
Example: Temporary shutdown of one regional facility and delay in inbound logistics
📝Workforce_Impact
InputText InputText
Description of the impact on employees and workforce availability.
Capture attendance drop, displacement, safety concerns, team redistribution, or wellbeing effects.
Example: 40 employees temporarily displaced and 18% attendance drop for 2 weeks
📝Capability_Impact_Area
InputText InputText
Capability domains affected by the event.
Identify what organizational capabilities were stressed, exposed, or found insufficient during the event.
Example: Business continuity, remote coordination, crisis leadership
📝Technology_or_System_Impact
InputText InputText
Impact of the event on technology systems or digital operations.
Useful for resilience planning where systems, networks, tools, or platforms were affected.
Example: Network outage and delayed ERP access
📝Customer_Impact_Level
InputDropdownText
Level of impact on customers or service continuity.
Use controlled values such as Low, Moderate, High, or Critical to summarize customer-facing disruption.
Example: Moderate
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝Financial_Impact_Estimate
InputText InputText
Estimated financial impact of the crisis or disruption.
Record a high-level monetary estimate to provide business context for the event.
Example: USD 180000
📝Response_Action_Taken
InputText InputText
Key response or mitigation action taken.
Capture the main action or intervention deployed to manage the crisis.
Example: Shifted key teams to alternate remote operating model
📝Leadership_Response_Effectiveness
InputText InputText
Effectiveness of leadership response during the event.
Used to assess leadership capability under disruption conditions.
Example: High
📝Recovery_Status
InputDropdownText
Current recovery or closure status of the event.
Use values such as Recovered, Partially Recovered, Stabilized, or Ongoing.
Example: Recovered
📝Lessons_Learned
InputText InputText
Key lessons identified from the event.
Capture what the organization learned from the disruption to improve future resilience.
Example: Need stronger site-level continuity planning and backup communication tools
📝Future_Preparedness_Action
InputText InputText
Preventive or preparedness action planned after the event.
Summarize follow-up action intended to reduce risk or improve readiness in future events.
Example: Launch crisis simulation drills and continuity training
📄Remarks
InputTextareaLongtext
Additional notes or contextual comments.
Use for contextual or exceptional notes that do not fit other fields.
Example: Triggered review of disaster recovery protocols

Skill Taxonomy

3 tabs · 29 fields
📝Skill_Domain
InputText InputText
Top-level grouping used to organize related skills into broad capability areas.
Use this to create the highest-level skills taxonomy in the platform. Domains should be broad enough to group multiple categories and knowledge areas, such as Digital, Leadership, Operations, Finance, Sales, Data, Compliance, or Customer Experience.
Example: Digital
📝Knowledge_Area
InputText InputText
Sub-domain grouping that clusters skills sharing a common body of knowledge.
Use this to break each skill domain into meaningful knowledge clusters. This helps organize assessments, learning content, role requirements, and analytics at a level below the domain but above individual skills.
Example: Data Management
📝Skill_Category
InputDropdownText
Functional grouping that classifies a skill based on type, use case, or business relevance.
Use categories such as Technical, Functional, Behavioral, Managerial, Domain, Digital, Compliance, or Analytical to support reporting and workforce planning.
Example: Technical
📝Skill_Name
PrimaryDropdownText
Standardized name of the individual skill being defined in the ontology.
Keep this name concise, unique, and reusable across roles, assessments, learning programs, and skill-gap analysis. Avoid duplicate naming for the same capability.
Example: SQL Querying
📄Skill_Description
InputTextareaLongtext
Plain-language description explaining what the skill covers and why it matters.
Describe the practical capability the skill represents, including what a person should be able to do when they possess the skill. Keep it business-readable rather than overly academic.
Example: Ability to write, read, and optimize SQL queries to extract and analyze structured data.
📝Proficiency_Level
InputDropdownText
Standardized five-level proficiency scale used to indicate the expected level of mastery for the skill.
Use the platform-wide controlled scale exactly as follows: Level 1 — Awareness; Level 2 — Developing; Level 3 — Proficient; Level 4 — Advanced; Level 5 — Expert. This keeps role mapping, assessment scoring, gap analysis, and learning recommendations consistent across the platform.
Example: Level 3 — Proficient
📄Skill_Proficiency_Description
Renamed from Level_Description
InputTextareaLongtext
Explanation of what performance looks like at the specified proficiency level.
Define what performance looks like for the selected proficiency level (Level 1 — Awareness, Level 2 — Developing, Level 3 — Proficient, Level 4 — Advanced, Level 5 — Expert), including expected autonomy, complexity handled, and quality of output.
Example: Level 3 — Proficient: Can independently apply the skill in regular work scenarios with minimal guidance.
📝Assessment_Method
InputText InputText
Recommended method used to evaluate whether an employee has the skill at the stated proficiency level.
Use methods such as manager review, practical test, certification validation, observation, simulation, self-assessment, peer review, or system-generated evidence.
Example: Practical test
📝Review_Frequency
InputText InputText
Expected interval at which the skill definition or proficiency requirement should be reviewed.
Use this to control governance cadence for fast-changing versus stable skills. The review frequency can be monthly, quarterly, half-yearly, yearly, or based on regulatory triggers.
Example: Annual
✅Emerging_Flag
InputToggleBoolean
Indicator showing whether the skill is new, fast-evolving, or strategically emerging for the organization.
Mark this as Yes when the skill is gaining importance because of technology shifts, market changes, transformation initiatives, or future capability priorities.
Example: Yes
✅Regulatory_FlagRequired
InputToggleBoolean
Indicator showing whether the skill has compliance, legal, safety, or regulatory relevance.
Use this flag for skills that must be maintained because of internal policy, audit expectations, external regulations, or mandatory certifications.
Example: No
🔑Skill_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique system identifier for the skill record.
Use a stable ID that does not change even if the display name of the skill is updated later. This ID should be used in role-skill mapping, assessments, training recommendations, and analytics outputs.
Example: SKL-001
📝Skill_Name
PrimaryDropdownText
Standard business name of the skill.
Keep the name clear, unique, and reusable across the platform. Avoid synonyms for the same skill unless one is defined as the official standard term.
Example: SQL Querying
🔑Skill_Domain_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Identifier of the domain to which the skill belongs.
Use this to link the skill to the relevant domain master or taxonomy layer so domain-level analytics and grouping remain consistent.
Example: SDOM-01
📝Skill_Domain
InputText InputText
Readable name of the skill domain associated with the skill.
This is the descriptive domain label used in dashboards and reports. It should align exactly with the domain used in the ontology and category structures.
Example: Digital
📝Skill_Type
InputDropdownText
Classification of the skill based on nature or application.
Use standard types such as Technical, Functional, Behavioral, Managerial, Domain, Compliance, Analytical, or Digital depending on the platform taxonomy.
Example: Technical
📄Description
InputTextareaLongtext
Business-readable description of what the skill enables a person to do.
Describe the capability in practical terms and avoid vague wording. This field should help role owners, assessors, and learners understand the skill consistently.
Example: Ability to query structured data, combine datasets, and extract insights using SQL.
✅Emerging_Skill_Flag
DependentToggleBoolean
Indicator showing whether the skill is strategically emerging or rapidly evolving.
Use Yes for new or fast-growing capabilities linked to digital transformation, AI adoption, automation, sustainability, or future business priorities.
Example: Yes
✅Regulatory_Skill_Flag
InputToggleBoolean
Indicator showing whether the skill has a regulatory, legal, safety, or policy-driven requirement.
Use Yes if the skill is needed to meet audit, safety, compliance, or legal obligations.
Example: No
✅Digital_Skill_Flag
InputToggleBoolean
Indicator showing whether the skill is part of the organization’s digital capability stack.
Use this to tag skills related to digital tools, systems, automation, platforms, analytics, cybersecurity, or digital operations.
Example: Yes
✅Green_Skill_Flag
InputToggleBoolean
Indicator showing whether the skill contributes to sustainability, environmental performance, resource efficiency, or ESG goals.
Use this when the skill supports green operations, sustainable design, environmental compliance, carbon reduction, or ESG reporting.
Example: No
📝Certification_Source
DependentDropdownText
Reference to an external certification body, internal credential, or recognized validation source linked to the skill.
Use this only when the skill is formally supported by a certification or validated learning standard. Leave blank if no certification source applies.
Example: Microsoft
📆Last_Reviewed_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Most recent year in which the skill definition or metadata was formally reviewed.
Use the latest completed year when the skill content, classification, or requirement logic was validated. This supports governance and taxonomy freshness tracking.
Example: 2025
🔑Category_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the skill category record.
Use a stable ID to uniquely identify each category so it can be referenced consistently in ontology, mapping, and reporting layers.
Example: SCAT-01
📝Skill_Domain
InputText InputText
Domain under which the skill category is grouped.
Use the official domain name so categories can roll up correctly into domain-level reporting and planning.
Example: Digital
📝Category_Type
InputText InputText
Name of the category used to group related skills within the domain.
Define broad, reusable categories that logically cluster similar skills. Avoid overly narrow categories that contain only one skill unless clearly justified.
Example: Data Analysis
📄Category_Description
InputTextareaLongtext
Explanation of what the category includes and how it should be used.
Describe the kind of skills that belong to this category and its practical meaning in workforce planning, role design, learning, and analytics.
Example: Includes skills related to collecting, interpreting, visualizing, and using data for decision-making.
📝Parent_Category
InputText InputText
Higher-level category to which this category belongs, if a nested category structure is used.
Use this only when the taxonomy requires multi-level grouping. Leave blank when the category exists at the top category layer.
Example: Analytics
📝Sort_Order
InputText InputText
Numeric sequence used to control how categories appear in interfaces, reports, or taxonomy views.
Use whole numbers to define display order. Lower numbers usually appear first. This helps maintain a clean and predictable taxonomy presentation.
Example: 1

Competency Framework

3 tabs · 19 fields
🔑Competency_Framework_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for a competency framework or library.
Use this when the organization maintains one or more formal competency structures, such as enterprise-wide, function-specific, leadership, technical, or role-family frameworks. This helps manage multiple frameworks without mixing them.
Example: CFW-001
📝Framework_Name
DependentDropdownText
Official name of the competency framework.
Use a clear, standardized framework name that can be referenced in reports, mappings, assessments, and governance documentation. The name should identify the framework scope rather than a single competency.
Example: Enterprise Core Competency Framework
📝Competency_Name
InputText InputText
Name of the individual competency included within the framework.
Use this field to record the specific competency that belongs to the framework. Each row should represent one competency under the selected framework, such as Communication, Strategic Thinking, Collaboration, Problem Solving, or Customer Focus. This makes the sheet usable at the competency level rather than only at the framework level.
Example: Strategic Thinking
📝Competency_Type
InputDropdownText
Broad type of competencies included in the framework.
Use a standard type to distinguish leadership, functional, behavioral, technical, digital, managerial, or compliance-oriented competencies. This supports filtering, reporting, and role alignment.
Example: Behavioral
📝Category
InputText InputText
Sub-group used to cluster related competencies within the framework.
Use categories to organize competencies into logical families such as Communication, Collaboration, Decision Making, Customer Orientation, People Leadership, or Strategic Thinking. Keep the structure broad and reusable.
Example: Collaboration
📄Description
InputTextareaLongtext
Plain-language description of what the framework covers and how it should be used.
Describe the purpose and intended use of the framework, such as role design, assessments, succession planning, learning recommendations, performance reviews, or career-path planning. Keep it readable for business and HR users.
Example: Defines the core behavioral competencies expected across the organization for role design, assessment, development planning, and talent decisions.
🔑Competency_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique system identifier for the competency record.
Use a stable ID that remains unchanged even if the competency name is revised later. This ID should be referenced in role-competency mapping, assessments, development plans, and analytics outputs.
Example: COMP-001
📝Competency_Name
InputDropdownText
Standard business name of the competency.
Keep the competency name clear, distinct, and reusable across the platform. Avoid vague labels or duplicate names that describe the same behavior in different words.
Example: Stakeholder Management
📝Type
InputDropdownText
Classification of the competency based on its nature or application.
Use standard types such as Behavioral, Leadership, Functional, Technical, Managerial, Digital, or Compliance. The chosen type should align with the competency framework and reporting needs.
Example: Behavioral
📝Category
InputText InputText
Grouping used to organize related competencies into a meaningful cluster.
Use categories to support navigation and analytics, such as Communication, Collaboration, People Leadership, Execution Excellence, Customer Orientation, or Strategic Thinking.
Example: Communication
📄Description
InputTextareaLongtext
Plain-language explanation of what the competency means in practice.
Describe the behaviors, judgment, or capability represented by the competency. Focus on observable and work-relevant meaning rather than generic dictionary wording.
Example: Ability to build, maintain, and influence relationships with internal and external stakeholders to achieve business outcomes.
✅Active_Flag
InputToggleBoolean
Indicator showing whether the competency is currently active and available for use.
Use this field to retire obsolete competencies without deleting historical records. Recommended values are Yes/No or Active/Inactive based on platform standards.
Example: Yes
Dropdown: Yes, No
🔑Competency_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Identifier of the competency to which the proficiency level belongs.
Use this field to connect each level definition to the correct competency record so assessments, role requirements, and development paths remain aligned.
Example: COMP-001
📝Competency_Name
DependentDropdownText
Readable name of the competency associated with the proficiency level.
This is the display label used in assessment forms, dashboards, and reporting. It should match the standard competency name defined in the competency master.
Example: Stakeholder Management
📝Proficiency_Level
InputDropdownText
Standard numeric or coded level representing expected capability maturity for the competency.
Use a consistent 5-level scale across the platform to support assessments and role benchmarks. Recommended scale: Level 1 — Awareness, Level 2 — Developing, Level 3 — Proficient, Level 4 — Advanced, Level 5 — Expert.
Example: Level 3
📝Competency_Proficiency_Label
Renamed from Level_Label
InputText InputText
Readable title of the proficiency level.
Use a short label that clearly communicates the maturity stage. This should align exactly with the approved proficiency scale and remain consistent across all competencies.
Example: Proficient
📄Competency_Proficiency_Description
Renamed from Level_Description
InputTextareaLongtext
Narrative definition describing what performance looks like at this level.
Explain what a person typically demonstrates at this level for the competency, including independence, complexity, consistency, and scope of impact. Keep it specific enough to support fair assessment.
Example: Consistently manages stakeholder expectations, communicates effectively, and resolves routine alignment issues with minimal guidance.
📝Observable_Behaviours
