InputText InputText
Examples of behaviors that indicate a person is operating at the stated level.
List concrete, visible actions or work behaviors that can be observed by managers, assessors, or peers. Avoid vague wording like “good communication” without examples.
Example: Builds working relationships, adapts messages to different audiences, and follows through on stakeholder commitments.
📝Assessment_Criteria
InputText InputText
Criteria or evidence used to evaluate whether the competency level has been achieved.
Use this to document how the level should be assessed, such as behavioral indicators, feedback evidence, examples from work, assessment center results, or manager validation.
Example: Assessed through manager observation, 360-degree feedback, and examples of successful stakeholder alignment in projects.

Role Architecture

4 tabs · 27 fields
📝Role_Family
InputText InputText
Broad grouping of related job roles that share similar capability themes or career tracks.
Use this field to cluster roles into logical families such as Technology, Sales, Operations, Finance, Human Resources, Customer Success, or Data. This helps organize role structures and enables family-level analytics, workforce planning, and progression mapping.
Example: Technology
📝Role_Title
DependentDropdownText
Standard name of the job role used across the organization.
Use a consistent, approved role title rather than informal or local variations. This title should be the primary role label referenced in role architecture, skill mapping, competency requirements, assessments, and reporting.
Example: Data Analyst
📝Job_Level
InputText InputText
Readable level name that indicates the seniority of the role.
Use the business-friendly level descriptor that employees, managers, and HR understand easily. This should align with the formal level architecture and be consistent across role families.
Example: Manager
📝Job_Level_Code
InputDropdownText
Short code used to uniquely identify the role level in systems and mappings.
Use a compact code such as L1, L2, M1, M2, IC3, or similar to simplify system integration, reporting, and requirement mapping. The code should map directly to the job role level master.
Example: M1
📝Department
PrimaryDropdownText
Functional department to which the role primarily belongs.
Use the standard department name to anchor the role within the organization structure, such as Finance, Marketing, Engineering, Operations, Procurement, or People & Culture.
Example: Analytics
📝Reports_To
InputDropdownText
Standard manager or parent role to which this role typically reports.
Use the expected reporting relationship at the role-design level, not the name of a specific person. This supports organization design, career-path logic, and hierarchy modeling.
Example: Analytics Manager
📝Role_Purpose
InputText InputText
Plain-language summary of why the role exists and what it is accountable for.
Write a concise description of the role’s core purpose, including the type of work performed, expected contribution, and business value delivered. Keep it readable for HR, managers, and employees.
Example: Transforms data into actionable insights through analysis, reporting, and business decision support.
📝Level_Code
DependentDropdownText
Unique short code that identifies the job level in the role hierarchy.
Use a standard level code that can be referenced across role architecture, compensation structures, approval workflows, and analytics models. Keep it stable and system-friendly.
Example: L3
📝Level_Name
InputText InputText
Readable name of the job level.
Use a clear level name that employees and managers recognize, such as Associate, Analyst, Senior Analyst, Manager, Senior Manager, Director, or Vice President.
Example: Senior Analyst
📝Band
InputText InputText
Broad compensation or leveling band to which the role level belongs.
Use bands to group multiple levels into a wider structure for compensation governance, role grouping, and talent planning.
Example: Professional
📝Grade
DependentText InputText
Formal grade used in the organization’s HR or reward structure.
Use the grade code or label used in payroll, HRIS, or compensation systems. This helps link role levels to pay structure and internal mobility rules.
Example: G7
📝Seniority_Tier
InputDropdownText
Higher-level grouping that describes the maturity or seniority zone of the level.
Use values such as Entry, Early Career, Mid-Level, Senior, Leadership, or Executive to support broad reporting and workforce segmentation.
Example: Mid-Level
📆Min_Years_Experience
InputYear PickerYear
Indicative minimum years of relevant experience typically expected for the level.
Use this as a guideline for role design and workforce planning, not as a rigid rule. It should reflect the usual experience threshold associated with the level.
Example: 4
📝Scope_of_Impact
InputText InputText
Description of the breadth of business, team, process, or customer impact expected at the level.
Explain whether the level influences a task, a team, a function, a region, or the enterprise. This helps differentiate similar roles across levels.
Example: Impacts team-level analytics, reporting accuracy, and business decision support.
📝Decision_Authority
InputText InputText
Summary of the degree of decision-making authority associated with the level.
Describe whether the role follows defined instructions, makes independent operational decisions, influences cross-functional outcomes, or sets strategic direction.
Example: Makes independent decisions within assigned work area and recommends improvements.
📝Typical_Span_of_Control
InputDropdownText
Typical size or range of direct team oversight associated with the level, where relevant.
Use this mainly for managerial levels. It may be numeric, a range, or a descriptive value. For individual contributor levels, use Not Applicable or a platform standard equivalent.
Example: 5-8 direct reports
📝IC_or_Manager
InputDropdownText
Indicator showing whether the level is for an individual contributor, people manager, or both.
Use standardized values such as IC, Manager, or Both to support career-path design, organization analytics, and talent planning.
Example: IC
📝Pay_Band_Label
DependentText InputText
Label of the pay band or reward grouping linked to the job level.
Use the approved compensation label for the level so it can align with total rewards, benchmarking, and workforce cost modeling.
Example: PB-3
📝Role_Title
PrimaryDropdownText
Standard job role for which the skill requirement is being defined.
Use the exact role title from the role architecture so skill expectations map cleanly to role design, workforce planning, assessments, and learning recommendations.
Example: Data Analyst
📝SkillRequired
InputDropdownText
Name of the skill required for successful performance in the role.
Use the standardized skill name from the skills master rather than free-text variants. Each row should represent one required skill for one role.
Example: SQL Querying
📝Required_Skill_Level
Renamed from Required_Level
InputDropdownText
Expected proficiency level the role should have for the skill.
Use the approved skill proficiency scale, such as Level 1 — Awareness through Level 5 — Expert. This defines the target level needed to perform the role effectively.
Example: Level 3 — Proficient
📝CriticalityRequired
InputDropdownText
Importance of the skill to the role relative to other required skills.
Use a standard criticality label such as Low, Medium, High, or Critical. This helps prioritize development actions, hiring focus, and gap interpretation.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝Role_Title
PrimaryDropdownText
Standard job role for which the competency requirement is being defined.
Use the exact role title from the role architecture so competency expectations remain aligned with role design, performance management, and talent decisions.
Example: Project Manager
🔑Competency_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier of the competency required for the role.
Use the stable competency ID from the competency master to avoid ambiguity when names change or similar competencies exist.
Example: COMP-014
📝Competency_NameRequired
InputDropdownText
Readable name of the competency required for the role.
Use the standard competency name from the competency master. Each row should represent one competency expectation for one role.
Example: Stakeholder Management
📝Required_Proficiency_LevelRequired
InputDropdownText
Expected level of competency maturity required for the role.
Use the standard 5-level proficiency model so role expectations can be compared against assessments and development plans consistently.
Example: Level 4 — Advanced
📝Criticality
InputDropdownText
Importance of the competency to the role relative to other competencies.
Use a standard importance label such as Low, Medium, High, or Critical to support prioritization in talent reviews, role design, and development planning.
Example: Critical
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical

Market Intelligence

2 tabs · 22 fields
📆year
InputYear PickerYear
Historical year for which regulatory skill-demand conditions are being captured.
Use one row for each of the last 5 completed years to show how compliance and regulatory capability needs have evolved over time. This is not just a reporting-year tag; it is a historical time-series field.
Example: 2024
📝regulatory_skill_demand
InputDropdownText
Overall intensity of regulatory or compliance-related skill demand during the year.
Use a standardized label or score to represent how strongly regulation drove demand for compliance-related capabilities across the organization or industry.
Example: High
🔢compliance_training_hoursRequired
InputNumeric InputNumber
Total hours of compliance or regulatory training required or delivered during the year.
Use this to show the training burden associated with regulatory expectations. The value may be captured at organization level or as an average per employee, depending on the model standard.
Example: 4200
📝top_compliance_skills
InputDropdownText
Key compliance-related skills that were most important during the year.
List the most relevant skills or skill themes, such as Data Privacy, Information Security, Internal Controls, Quality Compliance, Documentation Standards, or Regulatory Reporting. Keep the names generic and reusable.
Example: Data Privacy; Internal Controls; Regulatory Reporting
📝environmental_skill_gap
DependentText InputText
Summary of gaps in environment-related or sustainability-related compliance capability during the year.
Use this field to capture major capability shortages linked to environmental, health, safety, sustainability, or impact-reporting requirements, depending on the industry context.
Example: Moderate gap in environmental reporting and sustainability compliance skills.
📝technology_compliance_requirements
DependentText InputText
Major technology- or process-related compliance requirements that influenced skill demand during the year.
Capture the main compliance drivers linked to systems, cybersecurity, digital controls, auditability, data handling, or regulated process changes. This helps connect external requirements to capability planning.
Example: Increased need for cybersecurity controls, audit trails, and privacy-by-design practices.
🔑Signal_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the market-demand signal record.
Use a stable ID for each demand signal so market intelligence records can be tracked, updated, and referenced in downstream models.
Example: MSD-2024-001
📆Year
InputYear PickerYear
Calendar year associated with the market skill-demand signal.
Use the year component of the external or internal market signal. This supports time-series analysis of emerging, rising, or declining skill demand.
Example: 2024
📝Quarter
InputDropdownText
Quarter within the year for which the demand signal is captured, if applicable.
Use Q1, Q2, Q3, or Q4 when the market signal is tracked quarterly. If the source is annual only, follow the platform’s standard treatment for blanks or defaults.
Example: Q3
📝Skill_Name
PrimaryDropdownText
Name of the skill for which market demand is being tracked.
Use the standard skill name from the skills master wherever possible so external demand signals can be compared with internal capability and demand models.
Example: Machine Learning
🔑Skill_Domain_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Identifier of the broader skill domain to which the skill belongs.
Use the domain ID from the skill ontology so demand signals can be aggregated by domain such as Digital, Data, Cybersecurity, Operations, or Leadership.
Example: DOM-004
📝Signal_Source
CalculatedText InputText
Source from which the demand signal was derived.
Record the origin of the market signal, such as labor market analytics, job-posting data, analyst reports, industry benchmarks, or internal strategic research.
Example: Labor Market Analytics
📝Demand_Level
InputDropdownText
Current overall level of market demand for the skill.
Use a standard label such as Low, Moderate, High, or Very High, or a platform-defined score. This should reflect external demand intensity rather than internal need.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝Demand_Growth_YoY_PCT
InputText InputText
Year-over-year percentage change in demand for the skill.
Use a numeric growth percentage to show whether demand is rising, stable, or declining compared with the prior year. This helps prioritize emerging capabilities.
Example: 18%
📝Industry_Prevalence
InputDropdownText
How widely the skill appears across companies in the relevant industry or market.
Use a standardized label or percentage to indicate whether the skill is niche, growing, common, or near-universal across the target industry.
Example: Common
💰Salary_Premium_PCT
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Estimated market salary premium associated with the skill relative to baseline comparable roles.
Use a percentage where available to reflect the compensation premium driven by market scarcity or strategic importance of the skill.
Example: 12%
📝Geographic_Demand
InputText InputText
Key geographies, regions, or markets where demand for the skill is strongest.
Use this to highlight regional demand concentration, which can influence location strategy, hiring decisions, and talent mobility planning.
Example: India, Singapore, United Kingdom
📝Emerging_Technology_Driver
InputText InputText
Technology, trend, or business shift driving demand for the skill.
Record the main market driver behind rising demand, such as AI adoption, cloud modernization, cybersecurity threats, data regulation, automation, or sustainability transformation.
Example: Generative AI adoption
📝Strategic_Relevance
InputDropdownText
Importance of the skill to future competitiveness and strategic capability building.
Use a standard label such as Low, Medium, High, or Critical to indicate how strategically important the skill is for long-term workforce planning.
Example: Critical
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝Supply_Scarcity_Level
InputDropdownText
Extent to which qualified talent with this skill is difficult to find in the market.
Use a standard scarcity label or score to reflect talent supply constraints. This is different from demand level and is crucial for hiring and build-vs-buy decisions.
Example: High
📄Notes
InputTextareaLongtext
Additional context, caveats, or interpretation notes about the market signal.
Use this field for brief qualitative commentary, such as unusual market conditions, source limitations, or emerging patterns that help interpret the demand data.
Example: Demand is growing fastest in data product teams and AI-enabled automation roles.
✅Emerging_Skill_Flag
InputToggleBoolean
Indicator showing whether the skill is considered emerging, future-critical, or newly accelerating.
Use a Yes/No or True/False standard to identify skills that deserve special attention in future-skill planning and proactive learning strategy.
Example: Yes
Dropdown: Yes, No

Employee Master

3 tabs · 44 fields
🔑Employee_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the employee record.
Use a stable employee ID from the HR master so the person can be linked across assessments, performance, mobility, learning, and risk models. This should never be reused for another individual.
Example: EMP-000245
📝Full_Name
PrimaryText InputText
Complete official name of the employee.
Use the full name exactly as maintained in the HR system for reporting, search, and reconciliation with other source systems.
Example: Aarav Mehta
📝First_Name
InputText InputText
Given name of the employee.
Use the employee's first or preferred given name where the organization stores names in separate components.
Example: Aarav
📝Work_Email
InputText InputText
Official work email address assigned to the employee.
Use the active corporate email ID used for internal communication, system access, workflow notifications, and directory-based integrations.
Example: aarav.mehta@company.com
📝Phone
InputDropdownText
Primary contact phone number of the employee used for official communication.
Use the current work-related mobile or contact number maintained in the HR system, ideally in standardized international format where applicable.
Example: +91 98765 43210
📝Last_Name
DependentText InputText
Family name or surname of the employee.
Use the surname for identity matching, reporting, and integration with systems that store names separately.
Example: Mehta
📝Gender
InputDropdownText
Gender recorded for the employee in the source HR system.
Use the organization's approved gender values and ensure they align with DEI reporting standards and local compliance rules.
Example: Male
🔑Location_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Identifier of the primary work location associated with the employee.
Reference the Location_Master sheet so the employee can be mapped to branch, office, plant, hub, site, or any other operating location in a generic way.
Example: LOC-014
📝Location_City
InputText InputText
City of the employee's primary work location.
Use the city tied to the current or primary work location to support geographic workforce analysis and local talent planning.
Example: Bengaluru
📝Department
PrimaryDropdownText
Department or functional unit to which the employee belongs.
Use the standard department name from the organizational structure so employee analytics roll up consistently across functions.
Example: Data & Analytics
📝Role
InputDropdownText
Current job role or position title held by the employee.
Use the standardized role name aligned with Role_Architecture rather than an ad hoc title wherever possible.
Example: Data Analyst
📝Job_Level
InputText InputText
Seniority or grade level associated with the employee's role.
Use the defined job-level taxonomy so the platform can compare employees at similar responsibility and capability levels.
Example: Level 3
📝Education_Level
InputDropdownText
Highest completed education level or academically recognized qualification level of the employee.
Use standardized education levels such as High School, Diploma, Bachelor’s, Master’s, Doctorate, or equivalent so workforce capability segmentation is consistent.
Example: Bachelor’s Degree
📝Reports_To_Role
InputText InputText
Role title of the manager or supervisory position the employee reports into.
Use the reporting role or supervisor role to support org-structure analytics, leadership span, and succession mapping.
Example: Analytics Manager
📅Join_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Date on which the employee joined the organization.
Use the official date of joining from the HR source system. This supports tenure calculations, workforce planning, and early-tenure risk analysis.
Example: 2021-07-12
📆Tenure_Years
CalculatedYear PickerYear
Length of service of the employee in years.
Use a calculated or sourced tenure value based on the join date and current date or reporting cut-off. Keep the method consistent across the platform.
Example: 3.8
💰Annual_Salary_USD
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Annualized compensation value expressed in USD or the platform's normalized currency standard.
Use normalized annual compensation if cross-country comparison is needed. If local payroll data is used, document the conversion logic externally.
Example: 18500
📝Employment_Type
InputDropdownText
Employment arrangement of the employee.
Use standard labels such as Full-Time, Part-Time, Contract, Temporary, Apprentice, or Intern to support workforce segmentation.
Example: Full-Time
✅Critical_Role_Flag
InputToggleBoolean
Indicates whether the employee currently occupies a business-critical role.
Use Yes/No or True/False based on the organization's critical-role criteria so risk and succession models can prioritize this employee correctly.
Example: Yes
Dropdown: Yes, No
📝Employment_Status
InputDropdownText
Current active status of the employee within the organization.
Use a controlled employment-status value such as Active, On Leave, Suspended, Notice Period, or Inactive so workforce availability and headcount reporting remain accurate.
Example: Active
📆Total_Work_Experience_Years
InputYear PickerYear
Total number of years of professional work experience accumulated by the employee across all organizations and roles.
Captures the employee’s overall professional experience, including current and prior employment. Useful for workforce profiling, role-fit interpretation, mobility analysis, and succession context. Store as a numeric value in years, rounded consistently according to organizational rules.
Example: 8.5
📝Work_Mode
InputText InputText
Primary working arrangement assigned to the employee.
Use this field to capture whether the employee primarily works from Office, Hybrid, or Remote. This supports workforce planning, space planning, collaboration analysis, location dependency, and policy compliance checks. Store the actual employee assignment here, not just the policy definition.
Example: Hybrid
✅High_Potential_Flag
InputToggleBoolean
Indicates whether the employee is identified as high potential within the talent framework.
Use the organization's approved talent designation to support succession planning, mobility, and leadership pipeline analytics.
Example: No
📝Skill_Profile_Status
InputDropdownText
Current status of the employee's skill profile completeness or validation.
Use a controlled status such as Not Started, In Progress, Completed, or Validated to monitor skill-data reliability.
Example: Validated
📝Preferred_Career_Path
InputMulti SelectText
Career direction or pathway the employee has expressed interest in pursuing.
Use this to capture aspirations such as specialist, managerial, project, consulting, or cross-functional growth, where available.
Example: Analytics Manager Path
🔑Policy_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the HR policy record.
Use a stable policy ID so policies can be tracked across versions, audits, employee acknowledgements, and compliance workflows.
Example: HRP-001
📝Policy_Name
InputText InputText
Official title of the HR policy.
Use the approved policy name exactly as published so employees, managers, and auditors can identify the correct policy without ambiguity.
Example: Remote Work Policy
📝Policy_Category
InputDropdownText
High-level classification of the policy within the HR framework.
Use categories such as Leave, Attendance, Conduct, Compensation, Performance, Mobility, Learning, or Wellbeing for easy grouping and governance.
Example: Leave & Attendance
📆Effective_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year from which the policy version became effective.
Use the year tied to the current policy version or effective date to support historical policy tracking and review planning.
Example: 2024
📝Applicable_To
InputText InputText
Population or employee group to which the policy applies.
Use role groups, grade bands, geographies, employment types, or the label All Employees, depending on the policy scope.
Example: All Employees
📝Work_Mode_Rule
InputText InputText
Policy rule that defines allowed work arrangements and related conditions.
Use this field to specify whether employees may work in Office, Hybrid, or Remote mode and to describe the eligibility, approval, role suitability, location constraints, security requirements, or manager conditions attached to each mode under the HR policy framework.
Example: Office, Hybrid, and Remote modes are permitted based on role eligibility, manager approval, business need, and policy conditions.
✅Mandatory_FlagRequired
InputToggleBoolean
Indicates whether adherence to the policy is compulsory.
Use Yes/No or True/False to distinguish mandatory policies from guidance-only documents or optional frameworks.
Example: Yes
Dropdown: Yes, No
📝Review_Frequency
InputDropdownText
Expected cadence for formal review of the policy.
Use a standard interval such as Annual, Biennial, Quarterly, or As Needed so policy governance can be scheduled consistently.
Example: Annual
📄Policy_Description
InputTextareaLongtext
Short description of the policy and what it governs.
Summarize the intent, scope, and practical coverage of the policy so users understand what it addresses without reading the full document.
Example: Defines eligibility, approval flow, and usage rules for remote working arrangements.
🔑Compliance_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the compliance obligation or requirement.
Use a stable compliance ID so obligations can be linked to controls, audits, evidence, training, and risk monitoring.
Example: COMP-014
📝Compliance_Name
InputDropdownText
Name of the law, regulation, standard, or internal compliance requirement.
Use the official or commonly accepted title of the compliance requirement to maintain clarity across audits and governance reviews.
Example: Data Privacy Compliance
📝Regulatory_Body
InputDropdownText
Authority, standard-setting body, or internal governance owner associated with the requirement.
Record the external regulator, certifying body, or internal governance function responsible for issuing or overseeing the requirement.
Example: National Data Protection Authority
✅Mandatory_StatusRequired
InputToggleBoolean
Indicates whether the compliance requirement is mandatory, conditional, or recommended.
Use a controlled label such as Mandatory, Conditional, Recommended, or Voluntary based on the organization's legal and policy interpretation.
Example: Mandatory
📝Applicable_To
InputText InputText
Business units, employee groups, locations, processes, or functions covered by the compliance requirement.
Use this field to define scope precisely so compliance monitoring applies only to the relevant entities and populations.
Example: All customer-data handling functions
📝Key_Requirements
InputText InputText
Summary of the main actions, controls, or obligations needed to remain compliant.
Capture the essential compliance requirements in brief, such as documentation, reporting, controls, approvals, certifications, or safeguards.
Example: Maintain consent records, access controls, and incident reporting procedures.
📝Review_Frequency
InputDropdownText
Cadence at which the compliance requirement, controls, or evidence should be reviewed.
Use a standard frequency such as Monthly, Quarterly, Annual, or Event-Triggered to support monitoring and audit readiness.
Example: Quarterly
📝Evidence_Required
InputMulti SelectText
Type of evidence needed to demonstrate adherence to the requirement.
List the key artefacts expected during audit or internal review, such as logs, certificates, signed policies, reports, training records, or approvals.
Example: Access logs, policy acknowledgements, audit reports
📝Consequences_of_Non_Compliance
InputText InputText
Likely impact if the organization fails to meet the requirement.
Summarize possible outcomes such as fines, legal exposure, operational restrictions, reputational damage, or corrective action mandates.
Example: Regulatory penalty, reputational damage, and mandatory remediation plan
📝Risk_Level
InputDropdownText
Overall risk severity associated with non-compliance for this requirement.
Use a standardized rating such as Low, Moderate, High, or Critical so compliance issues can be prioritized consistently.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical

Performance Management

5 tabs · 78 fields
🔑Employee_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the employee whose yearly performance record is being captured.
Use the stable employee ID from Employee_Master so annual performance data can be linked to role, skills, mobility, learning, and succession records.
Example: EMP-1024
📆Year
InputYear PickerYear
Calendar or performance year for which the employee performance record applies.
Capture one row per employee for each completed year in scope, typically covering the last 5 years for trend analysis rather than a single reporting snapshot.
Example: 2024
📝Role
InputText InputText
Employee’s primary job role during the evaluation year.
Use the official role title or role code applicable for most of the year so performance can be compared meaningfully across similar positions.
Example: Business Analyst
📝Department
PrimaryText InputText
Department or function to which the employee belonged during the evaluation year.
Record the department aligned to the yearly review period so cross-functional performance analysis and internal movement tracking remain accurate.
Example: Product Management
📝Location
InputDropdownText
Primary work location or operating site associated with the employee during the evaluation year.
Use the standard location name or location ID to support location-wise workforce and performance analysis.
Example: Bengaluru Office
📈Performance_Rating
InputNumeric InputScore
Numeric or scaled overall performance score assigned for the year.
Use the organization’s approved performance-rating scale, such as 1 to 5 or 1 to 10, and keep the scale consistent across years.
Example: 4.2
📝Performance_Label
InputDropdownText
Descriptive label corresponding to the overall performance rating.
Use a controlled label that maps to the numeric rating, such as Needs Improvement, Meets Expectations, or Exceeds Expectations.
Example: Exceeds Expectations
📝Skill_Proficiency_Average
CalculatedText InputText
Average assessed skill proficiency of the employee for the year across relevant skills.
Use a computed or reported average from the employee’s validated skill assessments to connect skill strength with yearly performance.
Example: 3.8
📝Skill_Gap_vs_RequiredRequired
InputText InputText
Difference between required role skill level and the employee’s assessed skill level for the year.
Capture the average or weighted gap for the employee’s role-relevant skills so development need can be tracked year over year.
Example: -0.4
🔢Training_Hours_Completed
InputNumeric InputNumber
Total formal learning hours completed by the employee during the year.
Include completed internal or external learning hours only, excluding cancelled or incomplete programs.
Example: 42
📊Training_Completion_%Required
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of assigned or planned learning interventions completed during the year.
Use this to assess follow-through on development plans and mandatory learning requirements.
Example: 88%
📊Goals_Met_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of annual goals, KPIs, or OKRs achieved by the employee.
Capture achievement against formal goals for the year using the organization’s goal-tracking method.
Example: 91%
🔢Absenteeism_DaysRequired
InputNumeric InputNumber
Total number of workdays lost due to absence during the year, excluding approved long-term structural exceptions if required by policy.
Use this to support workforce health and productivity analysis while staying aligned to policy definitions.
Example: 6
📝Key_Development_Area
InputText InputText
Primary capability area identified for the employee’s improvement or growth during the year.
Record the most important development focus identified in the review discussion or development plan.
Example: Stakeholder Communication
📝Manager_Recommendation
InputText InputText
Manager’s summary recommendation based on the yearly review outcome.
Capture the action recommended by the manager, such as retain in role, promote, rotate, coach, or enroll in targeted learning.
Example: Prepare for next-level responsibilities within 12 months.
🔑Promotion_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the promotion transaction or record.
Use a stable promotion ID so each employee promotion event can be tracked separately for history, audit, and compensation analysis.
Example: PROM-2024-018
🔑Employee_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier of the employee who was promoted.
Link this field to Employee_Master to connect promotions with performance, skills, mobility, and retention history.
Example: EMP-1024
📝Employee_Name
InputText InputText
Full name of the employee who received the promotion.
Store the employee name for readability, but treat Employee_ID as the system key.
Example: Aarav Sharma
📅Promotion_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Effective date on which the promotion took effect.
Use the approved effective date rather than the announcement date so workforce history and compensation timelines remain accurate.
Example: 2024-07-01
📝Previous_Role
InputText InputText
Role held by the employee immediately before the promotion.
Record the formal pre-promotion role title or code to show role progression.
Example: Senior Analyst
📝New_Role
InputText InputText
Role assigned to the employee after the promotion.
Capture the formal post-promotion role title or code used in the HR system.
Example: Lead Analyst
📝Previous_Level
InputDropdownText
Job level or grade before the promotion.
Use the standard job level framework so vertical movement can be analyzed consistently.
Example: L3
📝New_Level
InputText InputText
Job level or grade after the promotion.
Record the approved new level to measure career progression and leadership-pipeline growth.
Example: L4
📝Department
PrimaryText InputText
Department or function in which the promoted role sits.
Use the destination department if the promotion included a functional move; otherwise use the continuing department.
Example: Business Intelligence
📝Location
DependentText InputText
Primary work location linked to the promoted role.
Use the employee’s main location at the time the promotion becomes effective.
Example: Hyderabad
📝Promotion_Type
InputDropdownText
Category of promotion event.
Use a controlled label such as Merit, Career Progression, Managerial, Fast-Track, or Lateral-with-Level-Increase.
Example: Career Progression
📝Approval_Status
InputDropdownText
Current approval status of the promotion request or action.
Use a governed workflow label so pending and finalized promotions can be tracked separately.
Example: Approved
📝Linked_Review_Cycle
InputText InputText
Performance or talent-review cycle that contributed to the promotion decision.
Record the relevant review cycle to connect promotions with talent discussions and calibration outcomes.
Example: FY2024 Annual Review
💰Previous_Salary_USD
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Annualized salary before the promotion, expressed in a standard currency if needed.
Use the approved pre-promotion fixed compensation value for compensation-change analysis.
Example: 54000
💰New_Salary_USD
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Annualized salary after the promotion, expressed in the same currency basis as the previous salary field.
Capture the approved post-promotion fixed compensation value for comparison and budgeting.
Example: 62000
📊Salary_Increase_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage increase in salary resulting from the promotion.
Use the approved compensation-change percentage so promotion economics can be analyzed across roles and populations.
Example: 14.8%
🔑Mobility_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the internal mobility event or transfer record.
Use a stable mobility ID so each role, department, or location move can be tracked independently.
Example: MOB-2024-041
🔑Employee_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier of the employee involved in the internal mobility event.
Link to Employee_Master to connect mobility with performance, skills, retention, and succession data.
Example: EMP-1148
📝Employee_Name
InputText InputText
Full name of the employee who moved internally.
Store the readable employee name while using Employee_ID as the system key.
Example: Priya Nair
📅Mobility_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Effective date of the internal movement.
Use the formal effective date of the transfer or movement rather than the request date.
Example: 2024-04-15
📝Previous_Department
InputText InputText
Department or function before the internal move.
Capture the originating department to analyze talent flows across functions.
Example: Customer Success
📝New_Department
InputText InputText
Department or function after the internal move.
Capture the destination department to understand internal mobility patterns and workforce allocation.
Example: Product Operations
📝Previous_Role
InputText InputText
Role held by the employee before the move.
Use the formal source role title or code from the HR system.
Example: Customer Success Specialist
📝New_Role
InputText InputText
Role assigned to the employee after the move.
Use the formal destination role title or code for clear movement tracking.
Example: Product Operations Analyst
📝Previous_Location
InputDropdownText
Primary location before the move, if the transfer involved a site change.
Use the standard location name or ID from the location framework.
Example: Chennai
📝New_Location
InputText InputText
Primary location after the move, if applicable.
Record the destination location to support workforce distribution and relocation analysis.
Example: Bengaluru
📝Mobility_Type
InputDropdownText
Type of internal movement.
Use a controlled label such as Lateral Move, Cross-Functional Transfer, Relocation, Temporary Assignment, or Growth Rotation.
Example: Cross-Functional Transfer
📝Reason
InputText InputText
Main business or career reason for the move.
Capture the stated reason such as capability development, workforce balancing, role redesign, employee request, or business expansion.
Example: Capability development and career-path alignment
📝Mobility_Action_Status
Renamed from Status
InputText InputText
Current lifecycle status of the mobility action.
Use a workflow status such as Proposed, Approved, In Progress, Completed, or Cancelled.
Example: Completed
🔑Exit_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the employee exit record.
Use a stable exit ID so every separation event can be traced for attrition, risk, and audit analysis.
Example: EXIT-2024-027
🔑Employee_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier of the employee who exited the organization.
Link to Employee_Master to connect exit outcomes with role, skills, tenure, performance, and succession data.
Example: EMP-1210
📝Employee_Name
DependentText InputText
Full name of the employee who exited.
Store the readable employee name while using Employee_ID as the primary linking key.
Example: Neha Verma
📅Exit_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Official last working date or effective separation date.
Use the approved final exit date because it drives tenure, workforce counts, and vacancy timing.
Example: 2024-09-30
📝Exit_Type
InputDropdownText
High-level category of separation.
Use a controlled label such as Voluntary, Involuntary, Retirement, Contract End, or Mutual Separation.
Example: Voluntary
📝Exit_Reason
InputDropdownText
Primary reason recorded for the separation.
Capture the most accurate reason available from HR records or exit interviews, using standard reason codes where possible.
Example: Career growth outside the organization
📝Department
PrimaryText InputText
Department or function the employee belonged to at the time of exit.
Use the final department assignment for department-wise attrition analysis.
Example: Engineering
📝Role
InputText InputText
Final role held by the employee before exit.
Record the last active role title or code to identify vulnerable roles or talent loss patterns.
Example: Software Engineer
📝Job_Level_at_Exit
Renamed from Level
InputDropdownText
Final job level or grade held at the time of exit.
Use the organization’s standard level framework to identify attrition concentration by level.
Example: L2
📝Location
DependentDropdownText
Primary work location linked to the employee at exit.
Use the standard location value to support geography-wise attrition and replacement planning.
Example: Pune
📅Join_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Original or latest valid join date used to calculate tenure.
Use the formal employment start date that aligns with tenure logic in your HR system.
Example: 2021-06-14
📅Exit_Date_Confirmed
InputToggleDate
Flag indicating whether the exit date has been formally confirmed and approved.
Use a simple Yes/No or Confirmed/Pending indicator so draft separations do not distort final metrics.
Example: Yes
Dropdown: Yes, No
📆Tenure_Years
CalculatedYear PickerYear
Employee tenure calculated between join date and exit date.
Use this field to support retention analysis and ensure the value aligns with approved HR date logic.
Example: 3.3
📈Last_Performance_Rating
InputNumeric InputScore
Most recent numeric performance rating prior to exit.
Capture the latest finalized rating to analyze whether high or low performers are being lost.
Example: 3.9
📝Last_Performance_Label
InputText InputText
Descriptive label corresponding to the employee’s last finalized performance rating.
Use the approved label from the latest completed review cycle.
Example: Meets Expectations
🔢Notice_Period_Days
InputNumeric InputNumber
Number of calendar or working days served as notice before exit, based on policy.
Use the organization’s standard counting basis to compare policy adherence and transition quality.
Example: 45
✅Rehire_Eligible
InputToggleBoolean
Indicates whether the employee is eligible for future rehire.
Use a controlled Yes/No or Eligible/Not Eligible status as defined by HR policy.
Example: Eligible
Dropdown: Yes, No
📝Exit_Process_Status
InputDropdownText
Current status of the employee exit workflow.
Use labels such as Initiated, Under Clearance, Completed, or Closed to monitor process completion.
Example: Completed
📝Backfill_Status
InputDropdownText
Current status of hiring or reassignment to replace the exited employee.
Use this to track whether the role has been backfilled, is under hiring, or has been deprioritized.
Example: Hiring in Progress
🔑Retirement_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the retirement forecast record.
Use a stable forecast ID so each employee’s retirement-readiness and timing record can be managed separately.
Example: RET-2025-011
🔑Employee_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier of the employee being evaluated for retirement timing.
Link to Employee_Master so retirement risk can be tied to role criticality, succession, and workforce planning.
Example: EMP-0844
📝Employee_Name
DependentText InputText
Full name of the employee included in the retirement forecast.
Store the readable employee name while treating Employee_ID as the system link key.
Example: Sanjay Kulkarni
📆Birth_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Employee’s year of birth used for age-based retirement planning.
Use the validated year of birth from the employee master or HR system, especially where retirement eligibility is age-driven.
Example: 1967
📝Current_Age
CalculatedText InputText
Employee’s current age at the time of forecast generation.
Use a current calculated or validated age value to support retirement-window planning.
Example: 58
📝Role
InputText InputText
Current role held by the employee.
Capture the present role title or code so critical capability exposure from upcoming retirements can be assessed.
Example: Plant Operations Head
📝Department
PrimaryText InputText
Current department or function of the employee.
Use the latest department assignment to understand retirement exposure by function.
Example: Operations
📝Location
InputDropdownText
Primary work location associated with the employee.
Record the standard location name or ID so geography-based retirement exposure can be tracked.
Example: Ahmedabad
📝Job_Level
InputText InputText
Current job level or grade of the employee.
Use the job-level framework to understand leadership and specialist retirement concentration.
Example: L6
📆Retirement_Eligibility_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the employee becomes eligible for retirement under organizational or statutory rules.
Use the approved rule set and do not confuse eligibility year with actual expected exit year if both are tracked separately.
Example: 2027
📆Years_to_Retirement
InputYear PickerYear
Estimated number of years remaining until retirement eligibility or planned retirement.
Use this field for workforce continuity planning and succession prioritization.
Example: 2
📝Critical_Role
InputToggleText
Indicates whether the employee currently occupies a role considered critical to business continuity or capability depth.
Use a controlled Yes/No or Critical/Non-Critical label based on the organization’s critical-role definition.
Example: Yes
Dropdown: Yes, No
📝Replacement_Pipeline_Status
InputText InputText
Assessment of whether a successor or replacement pipeline currently exists.
Capture the current readiness of internal succession or external talent pipeline for the role.
Example: Partial pipeline available
📝Risk_Level
InputDropdownText
Overall retirement-related workforce risk associated with this employee or role.
Use a controlled level such as Low, Medium, High, or Critical based on role importance and replacement readiness.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝Current_Action
InputText InputText
Immediate action currently planned or underway to mitigate retirement-related risk.
Record the specific mitigation action such as succession planning, mentoring, hiring, or cross-skilling.
Example: Accelerate successor readiness and document tacit knowledge

Workforce Planning

3 tabs · 47 fields
📆Year
InputYear PickerYear
Calendar or fiscal year for the workforce planning record.
Use one row per planning year. This field should support a multi-year workforce plan, not a one-time reporting label.
Example: 2026
📝Scope
InputText InputText
Organizational level or planning unit covered by the workforce plan.
Define whether the plan applies to the whole organization, a business unit, a function, a geography, or a location cluster.
Example: Technology Business Unit
📝Entry_Exit
InputDropdownText
Planning category indicating whether the row reflects incoming or outgoing workforce movement assumptions.
Use a simple controlled value such as Entry, Exit, or Net Movement when the plan separates hiring from attrition assumptions.
Example: Entry
🔢Opening_Headcount
InputNumeric InputNumber
Headcount available at the start of the planning period.
Capture the approved or expected opening workforce base before planned hiring, exits, transfers, or restructuring actions.
Example: 250
📝Planned_Hiring
InputText InputText
Number of hires expected to be added during the planning period.
Use projected approved hiring demand based on growth plans, backfills, new projects, or capability-building needs.
Example: 45
📝Expected_Attrition
InputText InputText
Number of employees expected to leave during the planning period.
Estimate expected exits using historical attrition, market pressure, retirement exposure, and business context.
Example: 18
🔢Projected_Closing_Headcount
InputNumeric InputNumber
Expected headcount at the end of the planning period after applying all planned workforce movements.
Calculate as Opening_Headcount + Planned_Hiring - Expected_Attrition. If transfers, retirements, or restructuring are tracked separately, include them explicitly in the logic and document the rule.
Example: 277 = 250 + 45 - 18
📝Net_Change
InputText InputText
Net increase or decrease in headcount during the planning period.
Calculate as Planned_Hiring - Expected_Attrition. A positive value means workforce growth, a negative value means workforce contraction, and zero means no net change. If internal transfers or restructuring are included in planning, adjust the formula consistently and document the treatment.
Example: +27 = 45 - 18
📝Hiring_Driver
InputText InputText
Primary business reason driving the planned hiring demand.
Use a concise reason such as growth, replacement, digital transformation, compliance demand, expansion, or new capability creation.
Example: Expansion of analytics and AI delivery capability
📝Critical_Roles_to_FillRequired
InputText InputText
Number or list of high-priority roles that must be filled to meet workforce objectives.
Use this field to highlight roles that are business-critical, scarce, compliance-sensitive, or delivery-dependent.
Example: 8 critical roles
📝Hiring_Confidence
InputDropdownText
Confidence level in the current hiring plan or forecast.
Use a controlled rating such as High, Medium, or Low based on budget certainty, demand visibility, and talent availability.
Example: Medium
📝Planning_Cycle_Status
Renamed from Status
InputDropdownText
Current status of the workforce planning record or planning cycle.
Use values such as Draft, Under Review, Approved, or Finalized so planning maturity is visible.
Example: Approved
📆Year
InputYear PickerYear
Calendar or fiscal year for the workforce health record.
Use one row for each completed historical year so the sheet supports trend analysis across multiple years.
Example: 2025
🔢Employee_Count
InputNumeric InputNumber
Total number of active employees in scope during the year.
Use the average or year-end employee count consistently across years for clean workforce trend analysis.
Example: 1240
📊Attrition_Rate_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Overall attrition rate for the workforce during the year.
Calculate the percentage of employees who exited during the year relative to the selected employee base.
Example: 12.4%
📊Voluntary_Attrition_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of employees who left voluntarily during the year.
Track voluntary exits separately because they often signal engagement, compensation, market pull, or manager-related issues.
Example: 8.1%
📝Avg_Age
InputText InputText
Average employee age for the workforce in scope.
Use this to monitor age-profile shifts, generational mix, and long-term workforce sustainability.
Example: 36.8
📝Median_Age
InputText InputText
Median employee age for the workforce in scope.
Median age is often more stable than average age when a few outliers skew the population.
Example: 35
📊Pct_Under_35
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of employees below age 35.
Use this as one indicator of workforce age distribution and early-career talent concentration.
Example: 46.0%
📆Avg_Tenure_Years
InputYear PickerYear
Average organizational tenure in years.
Measure the typical time employees have spent with the organization to assess experience retention and workforce maturity.
Example: 4.9
📆Median_Tenure_Years
InputYear PickerYear
Median organizational tenure in years.
Use the midpoint tenure to reduce distortion caused by a few very new or very long-serving employees.
Example: 3.8
📊Pct_Tenure_Over_10yr
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of employees with more than 10 years of tenure.
This helps identify long-tenured workforce concentration and potential retirement or succession exposure.
Example: 14.0%
📊Pct_L1_L2
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of employees in junior or early-career job levels.
Use this to monitor pipeline depth at entry and developing levels of the workforce structure.
Example: 38.0%
📊Pct_L3_L4
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of employees in mid-level professional or managerial roles.
This indicates the concentration of the workforce in core delivery, specialist, or team-lead levels.
Example: 49.0%
📊Pct_L5_Plus
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of employees in senior leadership or expert-level roles.
Use this to understand top-heavy versus pipeline-heavy workforce composition.
Example: 13.0%
📊Pct_Full_Time
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of the workforce employed on a full-time basis.
Track workforce composition by employment model to support cost, flexibility, and capacity decisions.
Example: 82.0%
📊Pct_Part_Time
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of the workforce employed on a part-time basis.
Use this alongside full-time and contractor mix to understand workforce flexibility and staffing design.
Example: 10.0%
📊Pct_Contractor
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of the workforce engaged as contractors or contingent workers.
This is useful for dependency analysis, workforce elasticity, and external talent reliance.
Example: 8.0%
🔢Training_Hours_Per_EmployeeRequired
InputNumeric InputNumber
Average learning hours completed per employee during the year.
Use structured learning hours such as instructor-led, digital, certification, and mandatory learning, using one consistent measurement basis.
Example: 18.5
💰Training_Investment_Per_Employee_K
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Average annual learning investment per employee, in thousands of currency units.
Use this to compare learning intensity across years, units, or benchmark companies. Keep the currency basis consistent.
Example: 1.2
📊Internal_Promotion_Rate_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of employees who moved into a higher role internally during the year.
This indicates internal career mobility, succession strength, and talent development effectiveness.
Example: 6.5%
📊Role_Required_Certification_Coverage_%Required
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of in-scope employees who hold certifications or credentials required for their roles.
Use a role-relevant certification coverage metric rather than industry-specific certification labels so the sheet remains cross-industry.
Example: 91.0%
📊Regulatory_Training_Completion_%Required
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of required employees who completed mandatory regulatory or compliance training.
Use this for any required annual, legal, policy, or standards-based training completion metric.
Example: 97.0%
📄Narrative
InputTextareaLongtext
Short commentary summarizing the workforce health trend for the year.
Capture key interpretation, such as strengths, warning signs, or changes that need action.
Example: Attrition improved, internal promotions increased, and compliance coverage remained strong, but mid-level tenure is declining.
🔑Forecast_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the workforce forecast record.
Example: WF-2026-008
📆Year
InputYear PickerYear
Calendar year for the forecast projection.
Example: 2027
📝Forecast_Type
InputText InputText
Type of workforce forecast. Use Headcount, Attrition, Hiring, Skill, or Combined.
Example: Combined
📝Department
PrimaryText InputText
Department or function being forecasted.
Example: Technology
🔢Projected_Headcount
InputNumeric InputNumber
Projected total headcount at the end of the forecast period.
Example: 285
📝Projected_Attrition
InputText InputText
Projected number of employees expected to exit during the forecast period.
Example: 34
📊Projected_Attrition_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Projected attrition rate as a percentage of headcount.
Example: 11.9%
📝Projected_Hiring_Need
InputText InputText
Projected number of hires needed to meet workforce targets.
Example: 52
🔢Net_Headcount_Change
InputNumeric InputNumber
Net change in headcount from the current baseline to the projected end state.
Example: +18
📝Skill_Gap_Forecast
InputText InputText
Summary of forecasted skill gaps expected in the projection period.
Example: Growing gaps in cloud architecture and AI/ML skills
📝Leadership_Gap_Forecast
InputText InputText
Summary of forecasted leadership pipeline gaps.
Example: Two critical senior roles without ready-now successors
📝Confidence_Level
InputText InputText
Confidence in the forecast accuracy. Use High, Medium, or Low.
Example: Medium
📝Data_Source
InputText InputText
Data sources and methods used to generate the forecast.
Example: Historical attrition trends, business growth plan, and skill demand signals

Leadership & Succession

5 tabs · 65 fields
📆Year
InputYear PickerYear
Calendar or fiscal year for the leadership capability record.
Use one row per completed historical year so leadership capability trends can be compared over time.
Example: 2025
📊Succession_Coverage_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of critical leadership roles with at least one identified successor.
Use this to measure how well important leadership positions are covered by succession planning.
Example: 84.0%
📈Managerial_Effectiveness_Score
InputNumeric InputScore
Composite score representing the effectiveness of people managers and leaders.
Build this from engagement, performance, retention, feedback, delivery, or other agreed leadership quality measures.
Example: 4.1/5
📝Leadership_Bench_Strength
InputText InputText
Overall strength of the available leadership pipeline relative to future demand.
Use a rating or index that reflects whether the organization has enough ready leadership talent to support continuity and growth.
Example: Strong
📈Avg_Leadership_Potential_Score
InputNumeric InputScore
Average assessed leadership potential of the identified leadership population.
Use a standard potential assessment scale so the score can be trended and compared across years.
Example: 3.8/5
🔢Critical_Role_Backfill_DaysRequired
InputNumeric InputNumber
Average number of days required to fill critical leadership roles when vacancies occur.
Shorter backfill times usually indicate stronger succession readiness and talent availability.
Example: 54
📈Succession_Readiness_Index
InputNumeric InputScore
Composite index showing the overall readiness of successors for key leadership roles.
Use a consistent formula that combines successor availability, readiness level, role criticality, and risk exposure.
Example: 78
📝Leadership_Programme_Status
InputDropdownText
Current status of formal leadership development initiatives during the year.
Use concise labels such as Not Started, Active, On Track, Delayed, or Completed.
Example: Active
📄Narrative
InputTextareaLongtext
Short interpretation of the leadership capability picture for the year.
Summarize strengths, gaps, succession risks, and major actions or observations.
Example: Succession coverage improved, but bench depth for senior technical leadership remains below target.
🔑Pipeline_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the leadership pipeline record.
Use a stable ID so each leadership pipeline entry can be tracked, updated, and audited over time.
Example: LP-2026-014
📆Year
InputYear PickerYear
Calendar or fiscal year associated with the pipeline view.
Use one row per level, department, or location for each year so pipeline readiness can be trended.
Example: 2026
📝Leadership_Level
InputText InputText
Leadership tier or level covered by the pipeline record.
Use this field for the broader leadership tier being tracked. Pair it with Leadership_Level_Code and Level_Label so both coded and descriptive reporting are available.
Example: Mid-Level Leadership
📝Leadership_Level_Code
InputDropdownText
Short code representing the leadership level or band in the organization.
Use standardized level codes such as L1, L2, M1, M2, D1, or E1 so leadership pipeline records can be grouped consistently across roles, functions, and years.
Example: M2
📝Leadership_Level_Label
Renamed from Level_Label
InputText InputText
Descriptive label associated with the leadership level code.
Use the human-readable level name mapped to the level code, such as Team Lead, Manager, Senior Manager, Director, or Vice President, to make pipeline reporting easy to interpret.
Example: Senior Manager
📝Department
PrimaryText InputText
Function or department associated with the pipeline record.
Use the relevant organizational unit to understand where leadership depth is strong or weak.
Example: Operations
📝Location
InputText InputText
Location, geography, or site associated with the leadership pipeline entry.
Use the geographic or organizational location if leadership planning is tracked by site, region, or hub.
Example: South Region
🔢Identified_Candidates_Count
InputNumeric InputNumber
Number of employees identified as potential leadership pipeline candidates.
Count all employees currently mapped as potential successors or future leaders for the defined scope.
Example: 16
🔢Ready_Now_Count
InputNumeric InputNumber
Number of candidates assessed as immediately ready for the target leadership role or level.
Use this to understand immediate bench strength for continuity or urgent vacancies.
Example: 4
🔢Ready_1_2yr_Count
InputNumeric InputNumber
Number of candidates expected to be ready within one to two years.
This shows medium-term leadership readiness after targeted development actions.
Example: 7
🔢Ready_3_5yr_Count
InputNumeric InputNumber
Number of candidates expected to be ready within three to five years.
Use this to monitor long-term pipeline depth and leadership sustainability.
Example: 5
📝Bench_Strength_Ratio
InputText InputText
Ratio of ready or identified candidates to the number of target leadership positions in scope.
Use a consistent formula so the ratio can be compared across units and years. Ratios below target signal pipeline weakness.
Example: 1.3
📝Gap_Status
InputDropdownText
Status showing whether the leadership pipeline is sufficient for the defined scope.
Use labels such as Healthy, Moderate Gap, High Gap, or Critical Gap based on pipeline thresholds.
Example: Moderate Gap
💰Development_Investment_USD
InputNumeric InputCurrency
Estimated or actual development investment allocated to leadership pipeline candidates, in currency units.
Use a consistent annual investment basis such as program cost, assessment cost, coaching, or development allocation.
Example: 45000
📄Notes
InputTextareaLongtext
Additional commentary about the pipeline situation, assumptions, or risks.
Capture context such as succession dependency, readiness concerns, or upcoming development actions.
Example: Two successors need cross-functional assignments before they can be considered ready-now.
🔑Plan_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the succession planning record.
Use a stable ID for each succession plan entry so the organization can track role coverage, reviews, and successor readiness over time.
Example: SP-2026-011
📝Critical_Role
InputText InputText
Role identified as critical for business continuity, strategic execution, or leadership stability.
Capture the specific role for which succession coverage is being planned. This should usually include leadership, specialist, or business-critical positions where vacancy risk is high.
Example: Head of Operations
📝Location
InputText InputText
Location, region, business unit, or organizational scope associated with the succession plan.
Use this field when succession planning is tracked by site, geography, legal entity, business unit, or operating location.
Example: South Region
🔑Current_Incumbent_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier of the employee currently holding the critical role.
Use the employee ID of the current role holder so the succession record can be linked to employee, risk, retirement, and workforce data.
Example: EMP-0041
📝Current_Incumbent_Name
InputText InputText
Name of the employee currently occupying the critical role.
Store the current incumbent name for quick review and interpretation. The employee ID should remain the main system key.
Example: Anita Sharma
🔑Successor_Employee_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier of the nominated successor for the critical role.
Use the ID of the employee identified as a successor. Multiple records may exist for the same critical role if more than one successor is tracked.
Example: EMP-0189
📝Successor_Name
DependentText InputText
Name of the identified successor for the role.
Store the successor name for easier review in planning discussions, while the employee ID remains the primary reference.
Example: Rohan Verma
📝Readiness_Level
InputDropdownText
Current readiness status of the identified successor to assume the role.
Use a standardized readiness scale such as Ready Now, Ready in 1–2 Years, or Ready in 3–5 Years so succession coverage can be compared consistently.
Example: Ready in 1–2 Years
🔢Readiness_Timeline_MonthsRequired
InputNumeric InputNumber
Estimated number of months required for the successor to become ready for the target role.
Use this field to quantify readiness in time terms. It helps compare candidates and plan development actions more precisely.
Example: 18
📝Development_ActionsRequired
InputText InputText
Key actions required to prepare the successor for the target role.
Capture targeted development steps such as mentoring, stretch assignments, leadership training, job rotation, certification, or project exposure.
Example: Cross-functional project assignment; executive mentoring
📝Succession_Priority
InputText InputText
Priority level assigned to the succession need for the role.
Use this to indicate urgency based on role criticality, vacancy risk, retirement proximity, or weak bench strength. Recommended values can include High, Medium, and Low.
Example: High
📅Last_Review_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Most recent date on which the succession plan entry was formally reviewed.
Record the last review date to support governance, review cycles, and auditability of succession planning decisions.
Example: 2026-01-15
📅Next_Review_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Next planned date for succession plan review.
Use this to enforce periodic updates and ensure successor readiness and role risk assumptions remain current.
Example: 2026-07-15
📝Plan_Status
InputDropdownText
Current status of the succession planning record.
Use a standard status such as Active, Under Review, Closed, or On Hold to show whether the succession record is current and actionable.
Example: Active
🔑Matrix_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the talent risk matrix record.
Use a stable ID for each matrix entry so performance-potential evaluations can be tracked, refreshed, and audited over time.
Example: TRM-2026-021
🔑Employee_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier of the employee mapped in the talent matrix.
Use the employee ID as the primary reference for linking the matrix entry to employee, performance, mobility, and succession records.
Example: EMP-0247
📝Employee_Name
InputText InputText
Name of the employee included in the talent matrix.
Store the employee name for readability in review discussions, while the employee ID remains the system key.
Example: Priya Nair
📝Role
InputText InputText
Current role or job title of the employee.
Use the employee’s current role so the talent review can be interpreted in the context of responsibility, scope, and career path.
Example: Senior Business Analyst
📝Department
PrimaryText InputText
Function or department to which the employee belongs.
Use this to segment talent reviews by function and identify talent concentration or risk areas within the organization.
Example: Technology
📝Location
DependentText InputText
Location, region, site, or organizational unit linked to the employee.
Use this field where talent review and risk discussions are tracked by geography, site, or business location.
Example: Bengaluru Hub
📝Job_Level
InputDropdownText
Current level, band, or grade of the employee.
Use the organization’s standard role level structure to support consistent comparison across employees.
Example: L4
📆Year
InputYear PickerYear
Calendar or fiscal year associated with the talent review record.
Use one row per employee for each completed review year so talent movement and risk patterns can be analyzed historically.
Example: 2026
📝Performance_Category
InputDropdownText
Categorized performance band assigned to the employee.
Use a standard banding such as Low, Moderate, Strong, or Outstanding based on the performance rating framework used by the organization.
Example: Strong
📝Performance_Label
InputText InputText
Descriptive label used for the employee’s performance position in the matrix.
Use a human-readable summary of performance strength. This can mirror the performance category or use the organization’s preferred label.
Example: Consistently Strong Performer
📝Potential_Category
InputDropdownText
Categorized potential band assigned to the employee.
Use a standard band such as Low, Moderate, High, or Exceptional to support 9-box or similar talent review frameworks.
Example: High
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝Leadership_Potential
InputDropdownText
Assessment of the employee’s potential for future leadership or broader role scope.
Use a standard label or score that reflects leadership capability, growth capacity, and future role readiness.
Example: High
📝Box_Position
InputDropdownText
Talent matrix placement based on the combination of performance and potential.
Use the organization’s standard 9-box names in this field. A common set is: Box 1 - Core Contributor (Low Performance / Low Potential); Box 2 - Inconsistent Performer (Moderate Performance / Low Potential); Box 3 - High Performer, Limited Growth (High Performance / Low Potential); Box 4 - Emerging Contributor (Low Performance / Moderate Potential); Box 5 - Solid Professional (Moderate Performance / Moderate Potential); Box 6 - Growth Talent (High Performance / Moderate Potential); Box 7 - Raw Potential / Misaligned Talent (Low Performance / High Potential); Box 8 - Future Leader (Moderate Performance / High Potential); Box 9 - Top Talent (High Performance / High Potential).
Example: Box 9 - Top Talent
✅Critical_Talent_Flag
InputToggleBoolean
Indicator showing whether the employee is considered critical talent for the organization.
Use Yes/No or True/False to flag employees whose loss would materially affect continuity, capability, or strategic priorities.
Example: Yes
Dropdown: Yes, No
📝Flight_Risk
InputDropdownText
Estimated likelihood that the employee may leave the organization.
Use a standard scale such as Low, Medium, or High based on retention indicators, manager input, and engagement signals.
Example: Medium
📝Flight_Risk_Driver
InputText InputText
Primary reason or signal contributing to the employee’s flight risk.
Capture the most relevant driver such as compensation, limited growth, manager issues, role stagnation, or external demand.
Example: Limited career progression
📝Recommended_Action
InputText InputText
Recommended retention, development, or talent action for the employee.
Use this field to record the agreed action from the talent review, such as promotion planning, stretch assignment, mentoring, role redesign, or retention intervention.
Example: Create internal mobility path and assign strategic project
📆Year
InputYear PickerYear
Calendar or fiscal year for the retention risk record.
Use one row for each completed year, and if needed by business unit or location, to track retention-risk patterns across the last several years.
Example: 2026
📝High_Risk_Employees
InputText InputText
Number of employees assessed as high retention risk during the year.
Count employees classified as high risk of leaving based on the organization’s retention-risk logic, signals, or review process.
Example: 42
📈Retention_Risk_Index
CalculatedNumeric InputScore
Composite retention-risk score representing the organization’s overall retention exposure for the year.
Use a standardized index derived from drivers such as flight risk concentration, voluntary exits, critical talent exposure, engagement signals, and talent market pressure.
Example: 68.5
📊Critical_Talent_Retention_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of identified critical talent retained during the year.
Calculate as retained critical talent divided by total identified critical talent for the year, multiplied by 100. Higher values indicate better protection of key talent.
Example: 91.0%
📝Average_Tenure
CalculatedText InputText
Average employee tenure for the measured population in the year.
Use average tenure to understand workforce stability and context for retention risk. This can be calculated overall or by scope such as business unit or role group.
Example: 4.8 years
📊Voluntary_Attrition_%
InputNumeric InputPercentage
Percentage of employees who voluntarily exited during the year.
Calculate as voluntary exits divided by average headcount, multiplied by 100. This is a core signal when analyzing retention risk trends.
Example: 11.4%
📝Key_Retention_Risk_Drivers
InputText InputText
Main factors contributing to elevated retention risk in the year.
Summarize the most material drivers such as compensation gaps, manager quality, burnout, low mobility, external demand, or skill scarcity.
Example: Pay competitiveness, limited growth, high external demand
📝Retention_Actions_Taken
InputText InputText
Major actions implemented to reduce retention risk during the year.
Capture the primary interventions taken such as compensation review, manager coaching, career-pathing, internal mobility programs, or targeted engagement actions.
Example: Critical-skill retention bonus; internal mobility campaign
📝Highest_Risk_Roles
InputText InputText
Roles or role groups facing the highest retention risk during the year.
List the most exposed roles so risk mitigation can be targeted where business continuity or capability risk is highest.
Example: Data Engineer; Sales Manager
📝Overall_Risk_Level
InputDropdownText
Overall qualitative retention-risk level for the year.
Use a simple overall level such as Low, Medium, or High to support executive review and trend comparison across years.
Example: Medium

Employee Relations

1 tabs · 27 fields
🔑Grievance_Record_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for each grievance record.
Use a stable, non-reusable ID for each grievance case to support audit trail and case management.
Example: GRV-0001
🔑Employee_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier of the employee raising the grievance.
Links the grievance to the employee record for workforce-risk, culture, and employee-relations analysis.
Example: EMP-0108
📝Employee_Name
InputText InputText
Readable name of the employee raising the grievance.
Included for review, validation, and operational case handling.
Example: Nisha Rao
📅Grievance_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Date on which the grievance was raised.
Used to track timelines, reporting periods, and case chronology.
Example: 2026-01-18
📆Grievance_Year
InputYear PickerYear
Year in which the grievance was raised.
Supports year-wise reporting and historical trend analysis.
Example: 2026
📝Grievance_Quarter
InputText InputText
Quarter in which the grievance was raised.
Supports periodic review and organizational hotspot analysis.
Example: Q4
📝Business_Unit
InputText InputText
Business unit of the employee raising the grievance.
Helps identify patterns or risk concentration by business unit.
Example: Shared Services
📝Department
PrimaryText InputText
Department of the employee raising the grievance.
Useful for manager-level, function-level, and team-level pattern analysis.
Example: HR Operations
🔑Location_IDRequired
PrimaryAuto GeneratedId
Location associated with the grievance.
Supports site or geography-wise grievance analysis.
Example: LOC-005
📝Grievance_Category
InputDropdownText
Broad category of the grievance.
Use a standard classification such as Managerial Conduct, Compensation, Harassment, Policy, Workload, or Facilities.
Example: Managerial Conduct
📝Grievance_Subcategory
InputText InputText
More specific subcategory of the grievance.
Adds finer detail within the grievance category for better analytics and corrective action planning.
Example: Unfair Treatment
📄Grievance_Description
InputTextareaLongtext
Brief description of the grievance.
Summarize the issue clearly without including excessive sensitive detail directly in the classification fields.
Example: Employee reported repeated exclusion from team decision meetings
📝Severity_Level
InputDropdownText
Severity or seriousness of the grievance.
Use controlled values such as Low, Medium, High, or Critical to support escalation logic.
Example: Medium
Dropdown: Low, Medium, High, Critical
📝Confidentiality_Level
InputText InputText
Confidentiality level assigned to the grievance.
Determines the privacy handling of the case and who may access the detailed record.
Example: Restricted
📝Grievance_Channel
InputText InputText
Channel through which the grievance was raised.
Examples include HR Portal, Ethics Hotline, Manager Escalation, Ombuds Route, or Employee Relations.
Example: HR Portal
📝Against_Function_or_Party
InputText InputText
Function, role, or party against which the grievance is raised.
Useful for pattern detection without necessarily capturing sensitive personal detail in free text.
Example: Immediate Manager
📝Case_Owner
InputText InputText
Person, team, or function handling the grievance.
Tracks accountability for review, resolution, and case closure.
Example: Employee Relations Team
📝Investigation_Status
InputDropdownText
Status of investigation or review.
Use values such as Open, Under Review, Investigated, Closed, or Escalated.
Example: Under Review
📝Resolution_Status
InputDropdownText
Status of final resolution.
Indicates whether the matter is resolved, unresolved, withdrawn, partially resolved, or escalated.
Example: Resolved
📅Resolution_Date
InputDate PickerDate
Date on which the grievance was resolved.
Used for closure analysis and turnaround-time measurement.
Example: 2026-02-03
🔢Resolution_TAT_Days
InputNumeric InputNumber
Turnaround time in days from grievance raised to resolution.
Measures responsiveness and grievance-governance quality.
Example: 16
✅Escalation_Flag
InputToggleBoolean
Indicates whether the grievance was escalated.
Useful for severity, governance, and pattern analysis.
Example: Yes
📝Corrective_Action_Taken
InputText InputText
Corrective or remedial action taken in response to the grievance.
Capture the primary action taken to address the issue, whether disciplinary, developmental, process-based, or advisory.
Example: Manager coaching and team-communication intervention
✅Repeat_Issue_Flag
InputToggleBoolean
Indicates whether the grievance is part of a repeat pattern.
Helps identify recurring employee-relations or culture issues that may need systemic action.
Example: No
✅Linked_Retention_Risk_Flag
DependentToggleBoolean
Indicates whether the grievance may contribute to retention risk.
Allows linkage between employee relations issues and retention-risk interpretation.
Example: Yes
📝Outcome_Satisfaction
InputText InputText
Reported level of employee satisfaction with the case outcome, if captured.
Useful for understanding whether formal closure also resulted in perceived resolution.
Example: Partially Satisfied
📄Remarks
InputTextareaLongtext
Additional notes or contextual comments.
Use for monitoring instructions, follow-up notes, or exceptional circumstances.
Example: Monitoring for recurrence over next quarter

Learning & Training

6 tabs · 110 fields
🔑Program_IDRequired
DependentAuto GeneratedId
Unique identifier for the training program.
Use a stable ID for each learning intervention so it can be linked to vendors, calendars, completion records, costs, certifications, and analytics across the platform.
Example: TP-2026-014
📝Program_Name
InputText InputText
Official name of the training program or learning offering.
Capture the formal program title used in catalogs, nominations, completion records, and governance reviews. Keep the name clear and reusable across business units.
Example: Advanced Data Analysis for Managers
📝Program_Category
InputDropdownText
High-level classification of the learning program.
